Tafseer:
1–4. Alif, Lam, Ra. [This is] a Book whose verses are perfected and then explained in detail from one who is Wise and Acquainted. 'Alir, Lam, Ra' is the Quranic name of this Surah. This same name was also given to the previous Surah, which is an indication that in terms of central theme and subject matter, both Surahs are closely related to each other. As you will see ahead, the only difference between them is of brevity (ijmaal) and detail (tafseel). The aspects that remained brief in the previous Surah have come forward with clarity and detail in this one. This difference is prominent in every aspect, but it will be seen particularly clearly in the narration of historical events. This method of detail after brevity adopted by the Quran is not merely for the sake of variety, but rather from the perspective of education, training, preaching, and calling (da'wah), this very method is beneficial and blessed.

The Concept of "Ihkam": "Ihkam" means to knot something well and make it strong. If a cloth is woven tightly and becomes dense, this word is also used for it. The use of this word for the Quranic verses is intended to reveal the reality that the teachings of the Quran were first revealed in the form of tightly-knit, concise, and comprehensive sentences, and then gradually they became clear and detailed. Accordingly, the Surahs revealed in the early Meccan period are perfect examples of brevity, comprehensiveness, and miraculous eloquence. The fundamental matters of religion in brief, tightly-knit sentences are like enclosing an ocean in a cup. Later, gradually, the color of detail was added to them, until in the Madani period, those same fundamental matters of religion became prominent in the form of a comprehensive and all-encompassing system of life. Referencing this is intended to draw people's attention to the special care that Allah Almighty has maintained in this Book for their education and training. It is worth remembering here that in the case of the Torah, this arrangement of sequence, gradualness, perfection, and detail was not maintained; rather, most of it was revealed all at once. At the end, there is a reference to the attributes of "Wise and Acquainted" (Hakeem and Khabeer) because only the Wise God could know how to enclose the treasures of wisdom in brief words, and then it was only the glory of the Acquainted God to reveal how many rivers and oceans are enclosed in a single cup.

The Fundamental Message of the Quran: "That you should not worship except Allah. Indeed, I am to you from Him a warner and a bringer of good tidings." This is the fundamental message of this Book: do not worship anyone other than Allah. This very message is the root of all religion and Shariah. The Messengers of Allah always came with this message as bringers of good tidings and warners. They gave the glad tidings of Allah's grace and mercy to those who accepted this message, and warned those who turned away from it of a dreadful punishment. Accordingly, this very duty of warning and giving glad tidings was now entrusted to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him).

Glad Tidings: "And that you seek forgiveness of your Lord and then repent to Him, He will let you enjoy a good provision for a specified term and give every doer of favor his favor." Here, the aspect of glad tidings is mentioned: seek forgiveness from your Lord and turn to Him in repentance, then Allah Almighty will grant you enjoyment of His blessings in this world until an appointed time, and He will bless every deserving possessor of virtue with His special grace.

Warning: "But if you turn away, then indeed, I fear for you the punishment of a great Day." This is the warning aspect: if you turn away from this call, then be prepared to face the punishment of a dreadful day. By this punishment is meant the punishment that inevitably befalls those who deny the Messenger. Those who accept the Messenger are granted a respite of life and achieve success, which remains as long as people remain upright on the correct path. But those who deny the Messenger are completely destroyed by a dreadful punishment once the respite of establishing the proof (itmaam-al-hujjah) passes.

The Two Pillars of Repentance: From this verse, the reality regarding repentance also comes forward, showing that it has two important pillars. One is Istighfar (seeking forgiveness) and the second is Tawbah (repentance). Istighfar is that a person begs Allah Almighty for forgiveness for his sin and vows to abstain from it in the future, and Tawbah is that he turns to Allah and adopts the correct path to which Allah has guided him. If a person does not abstain from the crime and does not adopt the correct path, then even if he utters repentance thousands of times with his tongue, his repentance is mere mockery.

"To Allah is your return, and He is over all things competent." The punishment mentioned above for those who denied the Messenger pertains to this world. Now, warning is given regarding the punishment of the Hereafter: after this, your return is to be to Allah, and He is all-powerful over everything. The warning hidden within the ambiguity of "He is over all things competent" cannot be contained in any explicit description.

Unquestionably, they fold up their breasts to hide from Him. Unquestionably, when they cover themselves with their garments, He knows what they conceal and what they declare. Indeed, He is Knowing of that within the breasts.

A Portrait of the Arrogance of the Proud: "Thani" means to turn, twist, or fold. It is a rule that when a person does not want to listen to something out of pride and hatred, he shrugs his shoulders, turns his chest, and walks away from there. This state has been described in Surah Al-Hajj, verse 9, as "bending his neck" (thaniya 'itfih) and here by "they fold up their breasts" (yasnoona sudoorahum). Another form of this is when a person gathers his cloak, wraps it around himself, and walks away; this has been described as "covering themselves with garments" (istighsha-e-thiyaab), as for example in Surah Nuh, verse 7: "And indeed, every time I invited them that You may forgive them, they put their fingers in their ears, covered themselves with their garments, persisted, and were arrogant with [great] arrogance."

Now, this verse depicts the attitude which the arrogant of the Quraish adopted in response to the warning of the Prophet, where they would turn their chests away out of pride and walk away from there, and in this way, in their own estimation, they thought they had secured themselves from Allah and His warning. One of human follies is that a person avoids facing a reality and thinks that now that reality is no longer a reality. However, someone's avoidance has no effect on the reality. If an ostrich senses a storm and hides its head in the sand, this does not change the direction of the storm. Similarly, if Allah is issuing a warning, then this device of hiding from Him by avoiding listening to Him is completely foolish. After all, where can a person hide from Allah? He sees people even when they wrap themselves in their garments. He is aware of the apparent as well as the hidden, and of all the secrets of the hearts.

And there is no creature on earth but that upon Allah is its provision, and He knows its place of dwelling and its place of storage. All is in a clear register. The terms "mustaqar" (place of dwelling) and "mustawda" (place of storage) have already been discussed under Surah Al-An'am, verse 98. By "mustaqar" is meant the dwelling place where man spends the days of his life. And by "mustawda" is meant the place where he is entrusted to the earth after death. This verse contains further explanation of the theme that passed in the verse above, which is that Allah's knowledge encompasses everything. No one can flee or hide from Him. It is said that He is the God at Whose hands every living creature receives its sustenance. The point is that He who reaches out with the destined provision to every living creature, wherever it may be—on the peaks of mountains, in the depths of seas, in dense forests, or in populated cities—how can anything be hidden from Him? Furthermore, there is a hidden reproach in this for those who try to hide from Allah, meaning: woe to these people who avoid listening to the words of Him on Whose provided sustenance they are being nourished! "He knows its place of dwelling and its place of storage"—He knows the dwelling place of everyone and also knows their grave where they are given as a trust to the earth after death. Using the word "mustawda" (place of deposit/trust) for the grave is an eloquent reminder so that no one thinks that once he dies, he is simply annihilated. He is not annihilated; rather, he is given as a trust to the earth, and a day will come when the earth will hand over this trust to its Lord. "All is in a clear register"—everything is recorded in a clear register; neither has anything been left out from being recorded, nor is there any trouble in searching for anything.

And it is He who created the heavens and the earth in six days—and His Throne had been upon water—that He might test you as to which of you is best in deed. But if you say, "Indeed, you are resurrected after death," those who disbelieve will surely say, "This is not but obvious magic."

Argument for Reward and Punishment: This is the mention of the reward and punishment with which they were being warned, but instead of accepting it and preparing for it, they used to mock it. It is said that the same God on Whose sustenance everyone is nourished is the One Who created the heavens and the earth in six days, and before this, His sovereignty was over water. We have clarified in another place that by "six days", our days are not meant, but rather divine days are meant, each day of which is equal to thousands of our years. We can express them as eras/phases. This gradual appearance of the world in six eras and reaching its point of perfection shows the reality that its appearance is not an accidental event, but rather its Creator gave it existence with intention, a plan, order, and wisdom. This intention, plan, order, and wisdom is a testimony to the fact that this is not a purposeless factory; rather, behind it is a grand purpose which must inevitably manifest. "And His Throne had been upon water"—the Throne is an expression of Allah's sovereignty. The meaning is that before the dry land of this earth appeared, this entire sphere was watery, and Allah's sovereignty was over it. Then dry land appeared from the water, various kinds of life came into existence, and step-by-step this entire world of existence became populated. The same thing is also mentioned in the Torah, though its translators have muddled the meaning. In the first verse of the Book of Genesis, the words are: "And darkness was upon the face of the deep. And the Spirit of God moved upon the face of the waters." "That He might test you as to which of you is best in deed"—that is, this entire arrangement and management is a clear testimony that this world is not a child's plaything or the game of a player, to be created in vain and to end in vain. Man, who holds the status of being the flower of this garden, and for whom it appears all these things were created, has not been left unchecked like an unbridled camel to eat, drink, enjoy, and end one day. If it were so, it would mean that the Creator of this world did a useless deed, whereas from every atom of this world, such testimonies of His power, wisdom, and mercy are found that in their presence, attributing any useless word to Him is entirely contrary to reason. If attributing such a thing to Him is contrary to reason, then it must be accepted that in this world, Allah Almighty is testing man by giving him freedom of will and the distinction between good and evil, to see whether he adopts the path of good or evil by his own choice, and inevitably he will one day be responsible and answerable before his Lord and will face reward or punishment according to his deeds. You will remember that the mention of the creation of the universe in six days also occurred in Surah Yunus. Here, the addition of "that He might test you as to which of you is best in deed" clarifies the reality that is implicit in creating this world with such elaborate preparation. "But if you say, 'Indeed, you are resurrected after death,' those who disbelieve will surely say, 'This is not but obvious magic'"—that is, this matter seems entirely self-evident and extremely clear, but if you explain this very thing to these people—that after death you will be raised for accounting and reward and punishment—then instead of considering your powerful speech and the beauty of your expression as the heart-touching nature of truth, they declare it to be the wizardry of words and speech, so that they can deceive their common people into not being influenced by the words of the Quran and the Prophet.

8–11. And if We delay the punishment from them until a determined period, they will surely say, "What detains it?" Unquestionably, on the Day it comes to them, it will not be averted from them, and they will be enveloped by what they used to ridicule.

The word "ummah" here is used in its exact literal sense, meaning a period of time, just as in Surah Yusuf: "And the one who was freed of the two remembered after a period of time (ummah)" (Yusuf: 45).

Response to the Mockery of the Disbelievers: In the verse above, mention was made of the mockery they were making of the news of being raised after death. In this verse, it is being stated that they adopt this same attitude of mockery towards the punishment with which they must inevitably deal in this world if they deny the Messenger. It is said that if We are postponing for some time the punishment of which they are being informed, then this is Our favor, that We are giving them respite for repentance and reform. But out of their wretchedness, instead of taking advantage of this respite, they think that this is merely an empty threat and mock the Prophet, saying that if the punishment is to come, why does it not come, what has held it back? It is said: alas for their audacity and wretchedness! On the day that punishment manifests, no one will have the power to avert it from them, neither will they be able to turn its direction away, nor will their partners and intercessors be able to help them in any way; that punishment will take them into its whirlpool, which they used to consider a joke and mock.

"And if We give man a taste of mercy from Us and then We withdraw it from him, indeed, he is despairing and ungrateful. But if We give him a taste of favor after hardship has touched him, he will surely say, 'Bad times have gone from me.' Indeed, he is exultant and boastful."

A Special Style of Speech: Here, although the word "insaan" (man) is general, it refers to the very people who are being mentioned above. When it is intended to turn away from an obstinate and argumentative addressee, this method is often adopted where, instead of addressing them or pointing towards them, the matter is stated in the form of a general principle (kulliyah). This fulfills the purpose of turning away from them and also makes the point more effective and powerful because of adopting the cloak of a general principle. This very style of speech has been adopted here, and extremely eloquent examples of this will follow. We will, God willing, discuss the benefits of this style of speech at an appropriate place.

The meaning is that at this time, when Allah has blessed them with His sustenance and grace, instead of being grateful for it, they are mocking His reminder and warning in their intoxication. When they are warned of punishment, instead of fearing, they obstitantly demand the punishment. Bear their state with patience and ignore them. Generally, the condition of people is that when Allah blesses them with His grace, they behave arrogantly and swagger instead of being grateful, and when they are caught slightly by God's grip, they immediately become broken-hearted and hopeless. Today, their pomp and pride is such that they do not care for anyone before them, but this pomp has no foundation; if the Divine decree shakes them just a little, see how they start wailing.

The Way of God's Beloved Servants: "Except those who are patient and do righteous deeds; those will have forgiveness and great reward." This is the description of those people who are exempted from the aforementioned general principle. These are the people who, when they are caught in any trial and affliction, remain patient and content in the hope of His mercy instead of becoming hopeless and broken-hearted, and when they are blessed with His grace and favor, instead of becoming arrogant and proud, they are grateful to Him and perform good deeds. Affliction strengthens their attribute of patience, and blessing opens for them the paths of gratitude and righteous deeds. These very people are the flower of humanity, and for them with Allah is forgiveness and a great reward. As for those who have gathered hopelessness from afflictions and pride and arrogance from blessings, they will become the fuel of Hell along with their accumulated stores.

Consolation to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): "Then would you possibly leave some of what is revealed to you and your breast be strained by it because they say, 'Why was there not sent down to him a treasure or there came with him an angel?' But you are only a warner. And Allah is Representative over all things."

This is consolation for the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) that: do not become disheartened by the attitude of these people and do not slacken at all in the discharge of your duty. If they make it a condition for accepting you as Allah's Messenger that you should have a very large treasure or that an angel should accompany you testifying to your messengership, then such foolish demands should not be a cause of worry for you. You have only been sent as a warner to them, not as a warden over them, that you must inevitably bring them to the path of guidance. After performing the duty of warning, your responsibility ends. If they want to avoid the truth by making futile demands an excuse, then hand over the matter to Allah. He is watching everything. Your hard work is before Him, and their mischiefs are also before Him. He will deal with them as He finds them deserving, and when He does, no one will be able to hold His hand. It should be kept in mind here that the reason for giving such instructions to the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was not that you were hesitating to preach any part of the divine revelation or wanted to leave it, but rather, in view of the intensity of the circumstances and your distress caused by them, it is an exhortation to you for steadfastness on your stance and an instruction to limit your responsibility to its boundaries. In such situations, the slight sharpness that exists in the address is not, as we have repeatedly shown, originally directed at the Prophet, but rather at those adversaries whose enmity is the cause of this guidance.

Challenge to the Opponents of the Quran to Present an Alternative Discourse: "Or do they say, 'He invented it'? Say, 'Then bring ten Surahs like it that have been invented and call upon whomever you can besides Allah, if you should be truthful.'"

This interrogative is of the nature of expressing astonishment. The objections above were narrated in a simple style, but declaring the Quran to be a book fabricated by the Prophet was an extremely strange thing, especially from the tongues of those who were critics of the beauty and ugliness of speech and appreciators of its conciseness and miraculous nature, and who also possessed a library of the works of their top poets and orators for comparison and competition. For this reason, its mention was made in the style of astonishment: if they shamelessly say this, then its decision can be made very easily by them presenting ten fabricated Surahs like it, and if this task is not within their power alone, they can also call for their help those partners and intercessors whom they worship besides Allah. In "if you should be truthful", one meaning is: if you are truthful in your claim that those whom you worship are Allah's partners. If they are Allah's partners, then this is the most important occasion for them to help you in presenting a match to the Quran, because due to this Quran, their own divinity is in the greatest danger. The second meaning within it is: if you are truthful in your assumption that this Quran is Muhammad's (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) own composition, which he falsely attributes to God to overawe people. In Surah At-Tur, it says: "Or do they say, 'He has made it up'? Rather, they do not believe. Then let them produce a statement like it, if they should be truthful." (Tur: 33–34).

The Nature of the Challenge (Tahaddi): In this Surah, ten Surahs have been demanded of them. In Surah Yunus, verse 38, a single Surah has been demanded. In Al-Baqarah, verse 23, a single Surah has also been demanded. In Bani Israel, verse 88, a match to the entire Quran is demanded, and in the aforementioned verse of Surah At-Tur, the words are "a statement like it", the clear meaning of which seems to be that a discourse like the Quran is meant, regardless of whether it is in the form of one Surah, ten Surahs, or a book like the Quran. Generally, keeping these different verses in view, people have established a sequence and order for this challenge: first, they were demanded to present a book like the Quran; when they were unable to do so, ten Surahs were demanded; when they fell short of this too, they were demanded to present a single Surah in the lowest degree, but they could not muster the courage for that either. Although this point seems good on the surface, its correctness depends on the period of revelation of the Surahs in which this challenge is mentioned being known with certainty. Since this matter is difficult, in our view, the correct opinion is that the Quran in the very beginning, as is clear from the aforementioned verse of Surah At-Tur, demanded they present a discourse like the Quran, whether in the form of ten Surahs or a single Surah, and later this brevity was clarified in different words according to the occasion. In our view, it is also not quite correct to understand this as a challenge in the general sense. The question of a challenge and dare arises where there is an assumption that the opponent has the courage to step into the field of competition and try their luck. When it is clear that all the arrogance of the opponent is merely an excuse to evade and escape from reality, then instead of challenging them with special preparation, it is better that the very first blow against them be a full and decisive one.

Establishing the Proof (Itmaam-al-Hujjah) upon the Opponents of the Quran: "And if they do not respond to you, then know that the Quran was revealed with the knowledge of Allah and that there is no deity except Him. Then, would you be Muslims?"

In the verse above, the matter was stated indirectly; in this one, they are addressed directly, saying: if these intercessors and partners of yours do not rise to fulfill your need in the campaign of presenting a reply to the Quran, then accept that this Book is the effusion of divine knowledge, just as the Prophet says, and not fabricated by him, as you allege. From "the Quran was revealed with the knowledge of Allah", it became clear that the real aspect of the Quran's miraculous nature is that the treasure of knowledge and wisdom it contains is a treasure that no one besides God can bestow. "And that there is no deity except Him"—this is the second self-evident conclusion that follows in the event of their inability to present a reply: that there is no deity except Allah, and those whom you have made deities are mere imaginary things that have no reality. If they have any reality, then this is the time for them to show some mettle to save your honor and their own. If even at this critical juncture their honor does not surge, then after all, what cure are they? In the style of expression of "Then, would you be Muslims?" there is an aspect of encouragement and invitation, as well as of restraint and reproach. The meaning is: you had full opportunity to prove your point, you could also call upon your deities for help in this, but if even after all this you could do nothing, then tell me, will you now become those who submit (Muslims)?

15–16. Whoever desires the worldly life and its adornment—We fully repay them for their deeds therein, and they therein will not be deprived. Those are the ones for whom there is not in the Hereafter except the Fire. And lost is what they did therein, and worthless is what they used to do.

Refutation of the Demand for Abundance of Sustenance: Above in verse 12, reference has passed to the taunt of the disbelievers where they used to present the lack of resources of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his companions as an argument against his messengership. Their assumption was that since we are in a far better state than them in terms of worldly causes and means, it means that we are also better than them in the sight of God; then what is the meaning of warning us of God's wrath and His punishment? If we were hated and accursed by God, would its result be that these people wander around wearing out their shoes while we enjoy ourselves? Then it should have been that they showered treasures and we straightened their shoes. In these two verses, their very misconception is answered. It is said that this world and its adornments are not given as a reward for righteous deeds, such that those who do not perform righteous deeds are deprived of them. This world is given to both the good and the bad; however, those who are seekers of this world alone, and have no concern for the Hereafter, their entire account is settled right here, and in the Hereafter, nothing remains for them except Hell. Since they do not perform any work with the Hereafter as their objective, all their deeds will go to waste in the Hereafter. The world without the Hereafter remains abstractly worthless, and whatever is done considering this worthless thing as the objective is all worthless, even if they apparently seem to be works of goodness. This theme has been stated at various places in the Quran. In Surah Bani Israel, this same theme has been stated as follows: "Whoever should desire the immediate—We hasten for him from it what We will to whom We intend. Then We have made for him Hell, which he will [enter to] burn, censured and banished. And whoever desires the Hereafter and exerts for it its due exertion while he is a believer—it is those whose exertion will be appreciated. To each [category] We extend—to these and to those—from the gift of your Lord. And never has the gift of your Lord been restricted." (Isra: 18–20). In these verses, from the words "what We will to whom We intend", this reality becomes clear: even the seekers of this world get only as much as God wills, and only those get it whom God wants to give. It is not that whoever becomes a seeker of this world can gather as much as he wants. In their case too, whatever happens is under God's choice and His wisdom. Similarly, the words "and exerts for it its due exertion while he is a believer" clarify this reality: the seeking of the Hereafter is also credible with Allah only when it is with faith and sincerity, and along with which there is practical struggle worthy of the Hereafter. If any pollution of showing off (riya) or association (shirk) is included in it, or if dreams of winning Paradise are being seen merely on the strength of verbal claims, then these delicious dreams have no value or worth with God.

So is one who is upon a clear proof from his Lord, and a witness from Him recites it, and before it was the Scripture of Moses to lead and as a mercy? Those believe in it. And whoever disbelieves in it from the factions—the Fire is his promised destination. So be not in doubt about it. Indeed, it is the truth from your Lord, but most of the people do not believe.

Now, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is being consoled: those who are inventing these excuses to evade and escape from the Quran, their real disease is that the light of their nature (fitrah) has been extinguished, and those whose light of nature has been extinguished cannot become believers in the Quran. Only those will believe in it whose nature is secured from being distorted. They certainly possess within themselves a proof and argument beforehand regarding fundamental realities, and then when after that a testimony also comes before them from above in the form of the Quran, they find it to be exactly the voice of their own heart. They feel that what they are finding in their hearts is being supported and confirmed by the tongue of this heavenly witness. In such interrogative sentences, the counter-sentence is sometimes omitted, which is understood from the context. It is as if the entire statement would be: are those who have these lights before them and those who are deprived of all these lights equal in the matter of believing in the Quran? This cannot be. Only those will believe in it who are blessed with the light of nature and are also familiar with the fact that before this, a similar book had descended upon Moses as a guide and a mercy. For such people, the Quran is certainly a familiar thing. When they hear it, they feel that what they were finding in their scripture of nature, its confirmation and support has also come before them from Allah through a witness. Accordingly, these are the very people who will believe in it. The meaning is: those who are making up various things in its opposition, the causes of their deprivation are present within themselves. The eyes of their hearts have gone blind. Do not expect any good from them.

By "Bayyinah" is Meant the Light of Nature: "Bayyinah" means a clear proof and obvious argument. Here, as we have indicated, it refers to that light of nature which God Himself has deposited within us for distinguishing between truth and falsehood, and good and evil. The more one's nature is secured from external bad influences, the stronger this light is within them. Accordingly, the Prophets are fully blessed with this. Their inner self is illuminated by this light even before being illuminated by the light of revelation. For them, the status of revelation is not of light over darkness, but rather, as has been said in Surah An-Nur, "light upon light" (noorun 'ala noor), i.e., light upon light. Ahead in this Surah, in verses 28, 63, and 88, this word has also appeared in this same sense. Among the common people, those who hasten to accept the call of the Prophets are also, according to their different ranks, blessed with this light, and the stronger this light is within someone, the more he hastens towards them and becomes their helper and strength. Entirely contrary to this is the state of those who, due to their wretched deeds, extinguish this light and get trapped in the whirlpool of "darkness, one above another" (zulumaatun ba'duha fawqa ba'd).

A Special Style Regarding Pronouns: Keep in mind here also that in the case of such words whose femininity is non-real, it is not necessary to respect the apparent gender of the word when bringing a pronoun for them; rather, a masculine pronoun can also be brought if the real referent of the word is masculine. Accordingly, in "yatloohu", a masculine pronoun has come for it, because what is actually meant by it is the very thing for which words like Noor (light), Burhan (proof), and Sultan (authority) have been used in other places of the Quran. "And a witness from Him recites it"—here "tala yatloo" means coming after and following something, and the referent of the pronoun in "minhu" (from Him) is Allah Almighty. Out of His grace, He first deposited within our nature everything to which the Quran calls, and then by His further grace, through the tongue of the unseen, He also made us hear this entire record so that no excuse remains for anyone. By "shahid" (witness) here is meant the divine revelation of which the Quran is the manifestation, the bringer of which is Gabriel the Trustworthy, and the bearer of which is the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon whom). Ibn Abbas has interpreted "shahid" as Gabriel the Trustworthy. In our view, this is correct. The difference between the revelation of the Quran, Gabriel, and the Prophet is only of expression. In terms of being a witness of God, there is no special difference between them. Accordingly, in the Quran, this word has been used for all of them.

The Testimony of the Past in Favor of the Quran: "And before it was the Scripture of Moses to lead and as a mercy"—that is, among the past testimonies, this testimony is also present before them for the confirmation and support of the Quran: that a similar book, with similar teachings and instructions, and indeed with predictions about it and its bearer, had descended before this upon Moses as a leader (imam) and a mercy. The words "imam" and "rahmah" (mercy) have come here in the same way as the words "huda" (guidance) and "rahmah" have come in some other places. Here, the concept of "huda" has been expressed by the word "imam" because the theme of guidance is equally present in both words. We have explained both of these words in another place, showing that they are used in terms of the beginning and the end. That is, this Book is the guidance and direction to the straight path in this world, and for those who accept this guidance and direction, it is the precursor of eternal grace and mercy in the Hereafter.

An Allusion to the Wretchedness of the Jews: A subtle allusion is also worthy of attention here. Since the addressees in this Surah are originally the Quraish, it might be assumed that the example of the Book of Moses would not be very effective for them. But this idea is not correct. The Quraish, firstly, acknowledged the people of the Book in terms of religious knowledge and merit; secondly, here the Quran also wants to make a slight allusion to those Jews who, by virtue of being the bearers of a book of "leadership and mercy", should have been the very first to hasten towards the Quran, but instead of hastening, they were secretly preparing conspiracies for its opposition. The Quran, in the form of an extremely subtle allusion, reminded them of what the demand of their rank and status was, and if they acted contrary to it, into what pit they would throw themselves. In "Those believe in it", the concept of restriction (hasr) is found. That is, only these people whose characteristics have been described above will believe in the Quran. From this, it follows automatically that those who are opposing the Quran are deprived of these characteristics. Since this point was clear from the context of the speech, it was omitted. "And whoever disbelieves in it from the factions—the Fire is his promised destination." In the word "ahzaab" (factions), there is some latitude. Along with its immediate opponents, the Quran has also made a slight allusion to those opponents who, though behind the scenes at this stage, emerged in later stages as extremely dangerous enemies—namely, the People of the Book. "So be not in doubt about it. Indeed, it is the truth from your Lord, but most of the people do not believe." Although this address can be general, in our view, the address is to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) himself. However, in such sentences, the address, as we have clarified in other places, is directed at the opponents, but they are no longer worthy of attention, so instead of addressing them directly, it is said to the Prophet. This style of restraint and reproach is sometimes more effective than direct restraint and warning. Extremely attractive examples of this are present in the Quran. The meaning is: if these wretched people are denying this clear reality, let them deny it; do not fall into any confusion because of their attitude. This very thing is the truth and it is from your Lord, but most people, due to their wretchedness, will not believe.

This theme has been stated in Surah Ar-Ra'd, verse 19. We will, God willing, explain it further there. Also cast a glance at what we have written under Surah Yunus, verses 94–95.

18–19. And who is more unjust than he who invents a lie about Allah? Those will be presented before their Lord, and the witnesses will say, "These are the ones who lied against their Lord." Unquestionably, the curse of Allah is upon the unjust. Who avert [people] from the way of Allah and seek to make it deviant, while they, in the Hereafter, are disbelievers.

Regret over the Wretchedness of the Polytheists: This exact theme, with this very context, has passed in Surah Yunus, verses 17–18. There, it is explicitly stated that what irritates the Quraish most about the Quran is its call to monotheism (tawhid). They would fly into a rage upon hearing the condemnation of their deities and would then blurt out whatever came to their minds against it. The Quran here, keeping in view their very original motive of opposition, has expressed regret over their wretchedness and deprivation: who can be more unfortunate and unjust to their own souls than those who invented a lie against Allah—that is, worshipped other things besides Allah and made this claim falsely, without any authority or proof, that God has made them His partners and commanded their worship? This is the greatest misfortune because this very thing will be the cause of their eternal deprivation when, on the Day of Resurrection, the secret will be revealed to them that those whom they worshipped throughout their lives and in whose defense they denied the Book of Allah have all vanished into thin air, and they are left to deal solely with the One, All-Conquering God. "And the witnesses will say, 'These are the ones who lied against their Lord.'" These witnesses can be the Prophets, because it is explicitly stated in the Quran that Allah Almighty will make every Prophet bear witness against his nation on the Day of Resurrection as to what he had taught them and what distortion they had created in it; they can also be those personalities who were worshipped as deities though they neither commanded it nor had any knowledge of it; and they can also be those angels who keep the record of the deeds of every person, and on the Day of Resurrection, this entire record will come forward. "Unquestionably, the curse of Allah is upon the unjust." This is the proclamation that will be made for the curse upon these polytheists after the testimony of the witnesses. And this curse will be the opening of all miseries for them.

The Crimes of the Polytheists: "Who avert [people] from the way of Allah and seek to make it deviant, while they, in the Hereafter, are disbelievers." These further attributes of the "unjust" have been described so that the speech becomes appropriate to the situation and their crimes which are the cause of this curse also come forward. One crime of theirs is that despite the truth being clear, they deliberately prevented people from the path of Allah, and the second crime is that they tried to crook and bend this path. The path for servants to reach God is entirely smooth and straight; there are no crooks, bends, or bypasses in it. If a servant walks on this path, he establishes a direct connection with his Lord. But these unjust people put many obstacles in this path. At every step, they turned its direction towards various stations, temples, goddesses, and gods, and in this way, they removed people from the original highway of monotheism and put them into side-streets and alleys. In "while they, in the Hereafter, are disbelievers", the repetition of the subject is intended for emphasis. That is, they are the real deniers of the Hereafter. Firstly, they tried to prevent creation from the path of Allah, and this audacity is not possible without being completely careless of the Hereafter. Secondly, shirk is itself the negation of the Hereafter, because when the partners and intercessors will somehow get their worshippers forgiven regardless of what their beliefs and deeds may be, then the Hereafter being or not being becomes equal. It is not required merely to believe in the Hereafter, but rather it is necessary to believe that it will be a day of uncompromised justice for everyone and no one's power will work before God on that day.

Those were not causing failure [to Allah] on earth, nor did they have besides Allah any protectors. For them the punishment will be doubled. They had not been able to hear, nor had they been seeing.

That is, the respite that Allah gave them in the world was not because they were out of God's control, but rather this was His leeway so that if they wanted to repent and reform, they could do so, otherwise they could fill up their measure well. Their assumption was also entirely baseless that they had some protectors and helpers who could save them from God's grip. Now all the realities have come before them. Now they will suffer the punishment for what they did, and since they did not merely stop from the path of Allah themselves but also prevented others, they will deserve a double punishment. "They had not been able to hear, nor had they been seeing"—that is, due to the intensity of hatred and aversion, they were neither prepared to listen to the words of Allah and the Prophet, nor did the veil of love for the world allow their eyes to open to see the signs of Allah.

Those are the ones who lost their souls, and lost from them is what they used to invent. Assuredly, they, in the Hereafter, are the greatest losers. It is obvious that those who wasted their capabilities in this way destroyed themselves with their own hands, and those on whose help and intercession they relied while playing this dangerous game will be of no use to them, because the imaginary deities they fabricated in their minds by inventing lies against God will all prove to be unreal. Obviously, the failure of the Hereafter will fall to the lot of such people who are deprived of intellect and insight.

The Virtues of the Believers: "Indeed, those who have believed and done righteous deeds and humbled themselves to their Lord—those are the companions of Paradise; they will abide eternally therein." Now, this is the mention of the end of the group opposite to the disbelievers and polytheists—namely, the believers. The meaning of "and humbled themselves to their Lord" (wa akhbatoo ila Rabbihim) is "they found peace in Allah and humbled themselves before Him". That is, cutting themselves off from everyone else, they bowed before their Lord with full peace of mind and complete single-mindedness. Above, the state of the polytheists was described that out of their pride and arrogance, they were neither prepared to open their ears nor their eyes, but these people adopted the path of faith and righteous deeds, and instead of removing God's creation from the straight path and making them wander in different valleys, they cast themselves before their Creator with complete single-mindedness and humility. It is said that these very people will be the inheritors of Paradise and will remain in it forever. Once they enter it, they will never be deprived of it.

The example of the two parties is like the blind and deaf, and the seeing and hearing. Are they equal in comparison? Then, will you not remember?

Above, the state of the disbelievers has been described: "They had not been able to hear, nor had they been seeing", meaning that the ears of their senses and the eyes of their insight had been closed, because of which they remained deprived of listening to the Quran and observing the signs of Allah. Contrary to this, the believers kept both their eyes and their ears open. It is said: the parable of these two groups is that one is blind and deaf and the other is seeing and hearing; how then can the attitude of both in the matter of the Quran be the same? The call of the Quran is entirely based on intellect and insight; only those will believe in it whose ears and hearts are open and who have preserved their bestowed natural capabilities.

The Subject Ahead. Verses 25 to 99: Ahead, up to verse 99, the stories of the past Messengers and their nations are narrated in a historical sequence. By narrating them, the objective on one hand is to show the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) that what you are facing today is exactly what was faced by the Messengers who came before you, so you should obtain guidance from their lives and face the circumstances with patience and determination just as they faced them. On the other hand, it is to show the Quraish that the path you have adopted with your Messenger is the very path your predecessor nations had adopted, as a result of which, after being given respite up to a certain limit, they were destroyed; so there is no reason why Allah Almighty should not deal with you in the same way as He dealt with them. Recite the verses ahead in this light.

"And We had certainly sent Noah to his people, [saying], 'Indeed, I am to you a clear warner.'" A brief reference to the story of Noah (peace be upon him) has also passed in the previous Surah, verses 71–73. In this Surah, the details of that brevity are coming.

The Meaning of "Nazeer Mubeen": In "nazeer mubeen" (a clear warner), there is an allusion to the reality that the wrath and punishment of which the Messenger warns is not of the nature of conjectures and indirect hints or metaphors, but rather a clear and definite news and proclamation, because its foundation, firstly, is on divine revelation. Secondly, this is also the demand of the divine sunnah (way) which inevitably manifests in the event of denial of the Messenger. The effect of this definiteness is naturally prominent in his words and tone as well. He proclaims the coming danger in such a way as if he is seeing it with his own two eyes. Here, in the word "mubeen", there is also a subtle allusion; keep it in mind as well. It was a custom in Arabia that when the people of any nation had a watchman on a high hill or mount where a guard was appointed at all times, his job was that when he saw a group of attackers coming from some side to attack his nation, he would tear his clothes, bare himself, and cry out "Wa sabahaha!" (O disaster in the morning!). This was an alarm for the entire nation, and everyone would unsheathe their swords and come out for defense. This was called the "naked warner" (nazeer-e-uryan). The Messengers of God also came to inform their nation of the coming punishment, and they informed people of it in such a way as if it was about to appear from behind them, which is why the words "nazeer mubeen" were used for them in the Quran.

The Call of Noah: "That you should not worship except Allah. Indeed, I fear for you the punishment of a painful day." This is the starting point of the call of Noah (peace be upon him). If you look back at verse 3 of this Surah, you will find that the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon whom) also initiated his call from this very point. Then, as you proceed further, you will find that the kind of opposition the nation of Noah made against Noah (peace be upon him) is the very kind of opposition the nation of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) made against him. This similarity of situations and events is what is being shown through these stories so that both the Prophet and his nation see the complete map of their present and future in the mirror of the past. The value of history lies in this very aspect. If this aspect is lost from sight, the status of history remains merely that of story-telling.

So the eminent among those who disbelieved from his people said, "We do not see you but as a man like ourselves, and we do not see you followed except by those who are the lowest of us at first glance. And we do not see in you any merit over us; rather, we think you are liars."

We have explained the research on the word "mala" in another place, showing that it refers to the leaders, chiefs, elders, and prominent figures of a nation. Since these people are the root of all corruption in a degenerated nation, they have had the greatest hostility towards the reformatory call of the Prophets. These leaders of the nation of Noah presented three objections simultaneously in response to the call of Noah. One was: "We do not see you but as a man like ourselves"—that is, you are just a human being like us. If God had to send a Messenger, He would have sent an angel or some other superior creature as a Messenger, or at least He would have sent an angel with you as a witness. What is the meaning of sending a human being like us as a Messenger to us? Look back at verse 12; the leaders of the Quraish said the exact same thing in response to the call of the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon whom). The second was: "and we do not see you followed except by those who are the lowest of us at first glance"—that is, your followers are only those among us who are base and of low rank, who have no honor or status in society, and who do not possess the ability to ponder over matters and analyze their results and consequences. They just heard your word and followed you without thinking. Al-Kashaf has taken "badiya-ar-ra'yi" in the sense of an adverb and clarified it thus: "at the time of the first occurrence of their thought", i.e., they did whatever came to their mind at the first instance, without taking the trouble to ponder over it. The third was: "And we do not see in you any merit over us"—that is, if you are so beloved to God that He sent you as a Messenger, then it should have been that gold rained upon you and your companions, you were the owners of treasures, and servants and retinue marched along with you; but we do not see any preference for you and your companions over us in any aspect; on the contrary, we see that our state is far better than yours and your companions', which is why we think you are merely imposing a false threat upon us.

He said, "O my people, have you considered: if I should be upon a clear proof from my Lord while He has given me mercy from Himself but it has been hidden from you, should we force it upon you while you are averse to it?"

Response to the Objections: Now, in this verse and the verse ahead, the response to each of the aforementioned objections is given. Noah first said: what I am presenting before you is based on two things. One is that light of nature ("bayyinah") which was already present within me, and the second is that divine revelation ("rahmah") with which my Lord blessed me. If the light of nature had been present within you too as it is within me, then this call of mine would have seemed to you to be the voice of your own heart and you would have accepted it as Allah's mercy. But the trouble is that due to your ingratitude and bad deeds, you have extinguished the light of your nature, as a result of which, under the law of nature, your hearts have been darkened and the ability to accept any guidance does not remain in them at all. Now, how is it possible that I should stick a thing upon you for the sticking of which there is no affinity remaining within you at all, and that too in a state where you are averse to its very name? Look back at what we have written under verse 17.

And O my people, I do not ask you for it any wealth. My reward is not but from Allah. And I am not one to drive away those who have believed. Indeed, they will meet their Lord, but I see that you are a people behaving ignorantly. This is Noah's response to the arrogance of the chiefs of his nation: if you are not prepared to listen to and understand my word, understand well that there is no loss of mine in this. The loss is entirely yours. If I were demanding some wage from you in exchange for what I am presenting before you, then I would have cared for your aversion—that you have not valued my commodity and my shop will close. But when I am not doing any trade, rather I am distributing it free of cost just as I received it free of cost, then if you do not accept it, you alone will be in loss. The reward I am to receive is upon Allah, and it will reach me. It has no connection with your rejection or acceptance. As for the matter that those who have accepted my call are humble and base people in your eyes, because of which you feel ashamed of being close to me, I am not prepared to drive them away from me to please you. They have believed in their Lord, and tomorrow they are going to meet their Lord with this very faith. If I drive them away from me today to please you, what face will I show to God tomorrow? The real decision of nobility and baseness is to be made with God. He alone will tell who is noble and who is base in His eyes. In my eyes, you are the ones who are involved in ignorance.

And O my people, who would protect me from Allah if I drove them away? Then, will you not remember? This is further explanation of the verse above: if I drive away these poor people who believe in Allah to please you, who will save me from God's grip tomorrow? "Then, will you not remember?"—that is, you have become so mad in the pride of wealth and status that none of you is granted the ability to ponder over this aspect of the matter.

And I do not say to you that I have the treasures of Allah or that I know the unseen, nor do I say, 'Indeed, I am an angel,' nor do I say of those upon whom your eyes look down that Allah will never grant them any good. Allah is most knowing of what is within their souls. Indeed, I would then be among the unjust.

This is the collective response to all the objections of the opponents: if you do not find anything supernatural in me, when did I claim to be above humanity? When did I say that I have the keys to the treasures of God, or that I know the unseen, or that I am an angel? I am not a claimant of any of these things. I am only the Messenger of God, and the message with which He has sent me to you, I am reciting it to you. My companions are poor and needy, because of which you consider them humble; and since in your eyes all value and worth belongs only to the world and its resources which you possess, you think that if there had been any aspect of good in this call I am giving, how could it be possible that these perpetually hungry and naked people would be blessed with it while you, who in your own assumption are the inheritors of all good and virtue, remain deprived! If your arrogance is that since they have not received the world, how can God give them any other good and virtue, then I am not prepared to support this arrogance of yours. The world is received by both the deserving and the undeserving, but the blessing of religion is always granted to those in whose hearts there is capability for it and whose nature is secured from being distorted. Allah knows best the state of the hearts and natures. For this reason, if I support your assumption regarding them, I would also make myself a companion of the unjust.

32–33. They said, "O Noah, you have disputed with us and been frequent in dispute of us; so bring us what you threaten us with, if you should be of the truthful." He said, "Allah will only bring it to you if He wills, and you will not cause failure [to Him]."

The Demand of the Nation of Noah for Punishment: When the chiefs of the nation of Noah were completely defeated in the field of debate and discussion, and Noah had established the proof upon them from every aspect and left no way of escape for them, they thought the final option for themselves was to demand of him the punishment of which he was informing them. Accordingly, they said: O Noah! Debate and discussion have been plenty; now, if you are truthful, bring that punishment with which you are threatening us again and again. Noah said in reply: bringing the punishment is in Allah's power; He will bring it when He wills. But remember, you who are demanding the punishment with such pomp, when the punishment descends, no one will be able to escape from God's control at that time.

And my advice will not benefit you—even if I should wish to advise you—if Allah should intend to put you in error. He is your Lord, and to Him you will be returned. The final stage of calling, advising, reminding, and exhorting is that the Messenger performs his duty, announces his exoneration, and hands over his deniers to that end which has been destined for them by God. This point was spoken by Noah (peace be upon him) at this very stage: now you have come under the law of God and are deserving of punishment because of your deeds; therefore, if He leaves you to go on the path of misguidance, then even if I advise you thousands of times, my advice cannot be effective. Now your matter is with Allah; He is your Lord and to Him is your return.

Or do they say, 'He invented it'? Say, 'If I have invented it, then upon me is my crime; but I am innocent of what [crimes] you commit.' In this verse, some people have understood the addressee to be the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon whom) and have considered it as a transition (iltifaat) in the middle of the story of Noah. Although there is a place for it being a transition, in our view, as we indicated above, this is the verse of the announcement of exoneration at the final stage of the call and exhortation. In this verse, Noah was directed: if the people of your nation think that what you are telling them is all fabricated by yourself, which you are falsely attributing to God, then stop this debate and say to them: if all this is my invention, then the responsibility for this crime is upon me, and if this is the truth and you are deliberately rebelling against it, then I announce my exoneration from the responsibility of your crime. We have clarified in another place that after the Messenger's announcement of exoneration, the decisive hour arrives for the nation.

36–37. And it was revealed to Noah, "No one will believe from your people except those who have already believed, so be not distressed by what they have been doing. And construct the ship under Our eyes and Our inspiration, and do not address Me concerning those who have wronged; indeed, they are to be drowned." After the aforementioned announcement of exoneration, Noah was informed through revelation: those among your nation who had the capability to accept your call to faith have already believed; now no one else is left to believe. All the butter that was in the milk has been extracted; what remains is only whey. So rest assured, do not be broken-hearted and grieved by what they have been doing so far. Now, according to the Divine sunnah, the time has come for the manifestation of God's court for them, and the Divine decision is that all of them will be drowned; so construct a ship under Our supervision and according to Our instructions for the safety of yourself and your believing companions, and beware, do not say anything to Me now regarding these unjust people; now they must inevitably be drowned.

And he constructed the ship, and whenever an assembly of the eminent of his people passed by him, they ridiculed him. He said, "If you ridicule us, then we will ridicule you just as you ridicule."

"Wa yasna'ul-fulka" (And he constructed the ship)—"ja'ala yasna'ul-fulka"—according to the well-known style of the Arabic language, the defective verb (fi'l-naqis) has been omitted before the aorist verb (mudari').

The Taunts of the Nation of Noah: Noah (peace be upon him) started making ships according to the instruction of Allah Almighty. The arrogant of his nation, who considered the threat of punishment to be merely empty boasting (God forbid), when they saw that a ship was also being made for safety from the punishment, they must have naturally attributed it to mental illness, and whenever they passed by, they would make plenty of taunts against him, and who can estimate what effect these taunts would have had on the hearts of Noah and his companions. But Prophets and their companions have to pass through such trials. From this, it is proved what degree of certainty they have regarding the unseen of which they warn people. It is as if they are seeing it with their physical eyes, even though they cannot show it to their opponents.

A Special Style of Expression: By "then we will ridicule you just as you ridicule", it is not meant that we too will throw such vulgar taunts at you as you are throwing at us. Rather, the meaning is: today, just as this action of ours is a cause of ridicule in your eyes, tomorrow your end will be a cause of increase in faith and peace of mind for us. Today you are laughing, tomorrow you will weep, and we will be happy and joyful upon the manifestation of divine help and grateful to our Lord. Sometimes, due to the demand of vocal harmony in a sentence, the same word is used but its meaning is different, as is in "Dannahum kama danoo" (We repaid them as they repaid). To be happy at someone's misery is not a good thing under normal circumstances, but upon those against whom the proof has been established in such a way as Noah and his companions established it against their nation, the descent of divine punishment is a memorable event of the victory of truth and the defeat of falsehood, to be happy about which is the very requirement of faith.

And you will know who will get a punishment that will disgrace him and upon whom will descend an enduring punishment. That is: currently you are mocking us and attributing this preparation of ours to mental illness, but soon the time is coming when you will see upon whom the disgracing and staying punishment descends. Here it should be remembered that punishment is of two kinds. One is that punishment whose purpose is to awaken and shake the heedless and the deniers so that they listen to the words of the caller, and seeing the signs of the danger of which he is informing them, if they want to be warned, they may be warned. The second is that punishment which descends to cut off the roots of the deniers of the Messenger after complete establishment of the proof. This is the decisive punishment which forever disgraces those who mocked the warning of the Messenger and attributed his admonitions to mental illness. This is not merely a gust of wind that came and went, but rather upon whichever nation and whichever valley it descends, it pitches its tents there, and its exemplary story remains preserved in the form of ruins and remains as well as in the pages of history so that coming generations may also learn a lesson from their end. Furthermore, this very punishment becomes the preface to that eternal punishment with which they will deal in the Hereafter.

[So it was] until, when Our command came and the oven overflowed, We said, "Load upon the ship of each [creature] two mates and your family, except those about whom the word has preceded, and those who believed." And he had not believed with him except a few.

"Faara yafooru" means to boil over and gush. This word is used for the boiling and overflowing of a cooking pot, and also for the gushing of a burning oven. Here, the idiom "faar-at-tannoor" has been used metaphorically to express that cyclonic, stormy flood that came upon the nation of Noah, due to which heavy rain fell and the water of the nearby seas also boiled over. Our teacher (may Allah have mercy on him), in the commentary of Surah Adh-Dhariyat, while clarifying the nature of the punishment of the nation of Noah, writes the summary of the discussion at the end: "From this detail, the reality became clear that upon the nation of Noah came a fierce and swirling windstorm from which heavy rain fell, the water of the nearby sea boiled over, and waves started leaping from every side. In this storm, Noah's (peace be upon him) vessel went and rested on Mount Judi."—Tafseer of Surah Adh-Dhariyat by Farahi.

By "amr" (command/matter) in this verse, and also ahead in verse 43, is meant that punishment which manifested by divine command. It is said: when the punishment came and the storm boiled over, We commanded Noah to load in this ship two of each kind, male and female, your family, and those who believed. "except those about whom the word has preceded"—that is, those among your family are not included in this who have already come under the divine decision. By this decision is meant that decision which Allah Almighty made known to Satan on the very first day in response to his ultimatum: that of the jinn and humans who follow you, I will fill Hell with all of them. The meaning is: those who have come under this eternal decree will remain deprived of boarding it; load the rest in it.

The Majority Deprived of Faith is a Heap of Filth: From "And he had not believed with him except a few", the objective is to point to the reality that this dreadful storm swept away the great majority of existing human beings, and only a few people who had believed in Noah remained safe from it. In this, the independence of Allah is also expressed—that in His eyes, that majority is a heap of filth which is deprived of faith, He prefers to see His earth clean of this filth and does not like to keep it loaded on the back of His earth merely on the basis that the quantity is large. Along with this, His boundless mercy for the believers is also expressed—that no matter how small their number may be, the All-Clement, All-Merciful God protects these gems in the lap of His mercy under all circumstances, so much so that even the flood of Noah cannot cause them any harm. From this perspective, there is a threat in this sentence for the arrogant majority of the Quraish, and an immense message of consolation for those few and oppressed Muslims who had believed in the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon whom) and were the targets of the oppression and tyranny of the Quraish in that era.

And [Noah] said, "Board it; in the name of Allah is its course and its anchorage. Indeed, my Lord is Forgiving and Merciful."

The Focus of the Believer: According to the command of Allah Almighty, Noah invited his companions to board the ship, and the first sentence that came out of his tongue on this occasion was: "In the name of Allah is its course and its anchorage..." This sentence is an expression of the reality that no matter how much importance causes and means may hold in themselves, the primary reliance of a believer is not on causes and means, but rather on the mercy and favor of the Merciful, Compassionate God. If His favor is accompanying, then in a wavy and stormy sea, even a broken plank of wood becomes a support for man, and if this favor is not accompanying, then even the grand Titanic becomes the prey of the waves in the twinkling of an eye and all the workings of science prove to be worthless. In our Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) instructing us to start every work with "Bismillah" (In the name of Allah), the real secret is this: our gaze should not remain stuck merely on the causes, but the Cause of causes (Musabbib-al-Asbaab) who is behind the causes should also remain in sight, because causes work only with His permission. At the end, the reference to the attributes of "Forgiving and Merciful" expresses the reality that just as reliance on mere causes is not permissible, similarly, one should not be proud of one's deeds either, no matter how good they may be; rather, the real reliance must be on the forgiveness and mercy of God alone, for who knows what weight whose deed will hold in the scale of God.

And it sailed with them through waves like mountains, and Noah called to his son who was apart, "O my son, come aboard with us and be not with the disbelievers."

The Final Test of Noah's (peace be upon him) Loyalty: The ship was sailing within stormy waves. Waves were rising as if black mountains were rising (this is a correct picture of the rising of waves in a stormy sea). In the meantime, Noah (peace be upon him) saw that his son (in the Torah, the name of this son has come as Canaan) was standing at some distance. Seeing him, fatherly affection surged. He called out to him: "O my son, come aboard with us and be not with the disbelievers." In this call, the spirit of affection and invitation were simultaneously blended. "O my son" is a very loving address from a father to his son, and "be not with the disbelievers" is, as it were, the final invitation: O piece of my heart, there is still an opportunity to leave the company of these disbelievers and join us. The form of "And it sailed" (wa hiya tajri) is for picturing the situation. Similarly, the words "who was apart" (wa kana fee ma'zil) also came for this reason, so that the entire scene comes before the eyes of the reader. Stormy winds are blowing. Torrential rain is falling. Waves are rising like mountains. Noah's ship is struggling against the buffetings of these waves, when suddenly, as the gaze rises, they see that at some distance, the young son is standing stunned and bewildered by the situation. After all, it was also possible that this scene was not shown to him; just as many other disbelievers drowned out of his sight, he too would have become the prey of some wave far away. But no, Allah Almighty willed that Noah should see the exemplary spectacle of his son's drowning with his own eyes. This was the final test of Noah's (peace be upon him) loyalty, from which an estimate can be made of how Prophets are made to pass through trials, but with the support of Allah, they remain steadfast in every trial. Furthermore, from this, the reality also becomes clear that when God's law is so uncompromised, even if Noah's son is disobedient, He squeezes his neck right before his father's eyes, then what of others.

[The son] said, "I will take refuge on a mountain to protect me from the water." [Noah] said, "There is no protector today from the decree of Allah, except for whom He has mercy." And the waves came between them, and he was among the drowned.

The Final Scene of the Dreadful Tragedy: The son was also very obstinate. Even after seeing this dreadful scene, there was no change in his arrogance. In response to his father's affectionate invitation, he said: no care, I will take refuge on some mountain; it will protect me from this water. Noah said: this is not water, rather it is the wrath of God, from which there is no protector today. Only he will be saved from it upon whom Allah Himself shows mercy. In the meantime, a wave came between the father and the son, and he too was drowned. This was the final scene of this dreadful tragedy. After this came before them, commands were immediately issued to the heavens and the earth that: enough, now the work has been completed.

And it was said, "O earth, swallow your water, and O sky, withhold [your rain]." And the water subsided, and the matter was accomplished, and the ship came to rest on the [Mountain of] Judi. And it was said, "Away with the wrongdoing people."

The earth was commanded to swallow its water. The sky was commanded to stop now. "Iqla'" also means stopping from some work. "O sky, withhold" (ya sama-u aqli'ee), i.e., withhold your rain. "And the water subsided" (wa gheed-al-ma'u)—that is, the risen water went down. "Ghada yagheedu" comes as both intransitive and transitive. "Ghada-al-ma'u" (the water subsided). "Ghad-al-ma'u" (He made the water subside). Here, it is used transitively.

By "Judi" is meant Al-Judi, the name of a peak in the Ararat mountain range. In the Torah, only Ararat is mentioned. The Quran has mentioned that specific peak where the ship went and rested. From this, the dreadfulness of the flood can be estimated, showing from where to where the water reached in a few days. If in a limited area, the rain is so heavy that all its outlets are unable to discharge its water, then the rising of water there is an inevitable matter.

Curse upon the Punished: "Away with the wrongdoing people" is a sentence of expressing hatred and curse. That is: good riddance! Curse be upon these unjust people! By "unjust" (zulm) here is meant injustice to one's own soul—that is, Allah had created them with extremely high capabilities, but due to their wretched deeds, they made themselves a heap of filth on God's earth, to clean which God had to send a flood.

And Noah called to his Lord and said, "My Lord, indeed my son is of my family; and indeed, Your promise is true; and You are the most just of judges."

A Special Aspect of the Eloquence of Speech: Noah made this prayer when he saw his son drowning, so apparently its reference should have come with verse 43, but due to the demand of the eloquence of speech, its mention was postponed. It is as if in the eyes of God, this person, despite being Noah's son, was so wretched that as long as God had not drowned him, He did not even like to bring Noah's prayer regarding him under discussion. The reason for this wrath is clear: in this world, if any human being can achieve the greatest bliss and good fortune, it is this; if he does not value it and rises as a Satan in the house of a guide, then as is clear from the very sequence of the speech, God declared this person to be the most hated. It is as if the real target of the entire flood was this very person; as soon as he drowned, the end of the flood was immediately announced.

The Fatherly Affection of Noah: Noah made this prayer being overcome by fatherly affection, merely taking advantage of the word "ahl" (family) which passed above in verse 40—that Noah was commanded: "load in this ship of each kind, male and female, and your family, except those about whom the word has preceded." Since in the word "ahl", his son Canaan was also apparently included in the literal sense, and this matter was not known with certainty to his knowledge—that he had come under that decision of God, the reference to which we have given above while clarifying "except those about whom the word has preceded"—for this reason, he cried out: O Lord, my this son is also included in my family, and Your promise that You will save my family through this ship is a true promise, and You are the best of judges. The meaning is: when this true promise of Yours is present, why did this son of mine drown?

He said, "O Noah, indeed he is not of your family; indeed, he is [one whose] work was other than righteous, so ask Me not for that about which you have no knowledge. Indeed, I advise you lest you be among the ignorant."

A Prophet's Household is Formed by Faith and Righteous Deeds: Allah Almighty said in reply: this son is not included in your family for whom there was a promise of safety. The promise of safety was only for the believers, and of your family, only those were included in it who were associated with you through the bond of faith. "Indeed, he is [one whose] work was other than righteous"—this is the same style of expression as when it is said "Zayd is justice" (Zayd is personified justice)—that is, how can this person be counted in your family? He was entirely wretched and unfit. A Prophet's household is not formed merely by lineage, but rather by faith and righteous deeds. He was included in those about whom Our decision had been passed—that We will fill Hell with all such people. So do not request of Us for any matter about which you have no knowledge. I advise you lest you become among those who are overcome by emotions. We have explained the research on the word "jahl" in another place, showing that its original meaning in Arabic is to be overcome by emotions.

He said, "My Lord, indeed I seek refuge in You from asking You for that about which I have no knowledge. And unless You forgive me and have mercy upon me, I will be among the losers." Noah repented immediately after this warning, and Allah Almighty accepted his repentance.

It was said, "O Noah, disembark in security from Us and blessings upon you and upon nations [descending] from those with you. But other nations We will grant enjoyment; then there will touch them from Us a painful punishment."

Glad Tidings of Blessings to Noah (peace be upon him): After the flood passed, Noah was directed: now disembark upon the earth under the shadow of security and blessings from God. It is worth remembering here that those who attain safety by virtue of their faith and their determination from within any punished nation, since they emerge from the furnaces of trial completely clean of all kinds of dross and deceit, the divine mercy turns towards them in a special way and nurtures them under the shadow of His special blessings and favors. Their example is of extremely healthy seeds which grow in fertile land and favorable climate. For this reason, although their number may be small, they very soon cover all horizons. "and upon nations from those with you"—that is, today apparently there are only a few souls with you, but since God's mercy and blessing are upon them, great nations and communities are implicit in them, which will eventually manifest and spread over the entire face of the earth. Accordingly, history is witness that after the flood of Noah, these very souls became the means of repopulating the earth.

A Warning: "But other nations We will grant enjoyment; then there will touch them from Us a painful punishment"—along with this, they were informed of the actual fact that of the nations that will arise from their progeny in the future, not all will be blessed; rather, there will also be such nations among them who will attain rise, and We will also give them respite up to a certain limit, but eventually, in retribution for their deeds, We will catch them in a painful punishment.

That is from the news of the unseen which We reveal to you, [O Muhammad]. You knew it not, neither you nor your people, before this. So be patient; indeed, the [best] outcome is for the righteous.

Turning towards the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon whom): This is a turn towards the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) at the end of the story—that this story was neither in your knowledge nor in your nation's. This is merely Allah's favor that He informed you of it through His revelation, and through your medium, He provided the means for your nation to be informed of it as well. It should remain clear here that this story, in this clear form, along with its benefits and results, was narrated for the first time in the Quran. Before this, some parts of it were indeed narrated in the Torah, but in an extremely scattered and distorted form. Then, the Holy Prophet (peace be upon him) was unaware of the past scriptures, and so were the people of your nation. This is the grace of the Quran that it informed of these realities of history, and these realities, just as they were lesson-giving for the Prophet and his companions, were also so for your nation and you. "So be patient; indeed, the outcome is for the righteous." This is the summary of this story. The meaning is: remain firm on your call. Ultimately, success is achieved only by those who fear God. Your opponents will be unsuccessful and disappointed.
