And to 'Aad [We sent] their brother Hud. He said, "O my people, worship Allah; you have no deity other than Him. You are not but inventors [of falsehood]."

The Nation of 'Aad and Hud (peace be upon him): Regarding the Messengers, this has been the divine sunnah: to every nation, Allah Almighty sent a Messenger from among themselves, so that an excellent man from among them, in their own language, establishes the proof upon them, and national and linguistic estrangement does not come in between. In this way, as it were, one's own tongue and one's own heart bear witness against oneself, and one's own brother performs the right of advising, which should be valued by every person who has respect for the truth. Through the word "their brother" (akhahum), allusion is intended to this very reality: We sent to 'Aad not any stranger, but rather one of their own brothers as a Messenger, but in the heat of hostility to the truth, they made this very thing, as details will follow, an excuse for his opposition. "worship Allah; you have no deity other than Him." This call to monotheism has been the common call of all Prophets from the very beginning, and this was the call raised by the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon whom) as well. "You are not but inventors"—that is, if you make these claims that there are some other deities whom God has made His partners, then you are inventing a lie and slander against God. God has nowhere said that He has made anyone His partner.

O my people, I do not ask you for it any reward. My reward is only from the One who created me. Then, will you not use reason?

The Disinterestedness of Hud (peace be upon him): That is: I, who am explaining to you with such devotion, do not do so because I associate some personal interest of mine with it in return. I am not wandering behind you with some trade merchandise, such that if you do not buy, my business will close. This is merely the desire for your welfare and success which does not let me sit in peace despite all your lack of appreciation, so for God's sake, listen to my word with attention. After all, I am not asking for any recompense for it that you are not even tolerant of listening to my word. As far as my reward is concerned, it is upon my Lord Who created me. I want to give you something, I am not desirous of taking anything. Is such a clear thing not entering your understanding?

And O my people, ask forgiveness of your Lord and then repent to Him. He will send [rain from] the sky upon you in abundance and increase you in strength [added] to your strength. And do not turn away, [being] criminals.

The Call to Repentance and its Blessings: This is the invitation to the nation to repent—that repenting from shirk and disobedience, establish your relationship purely with your Lord. We have clarified in another place that repentance has two pillars: one negative, the second positive. The negative is that a person relinquishes the wrong beliefs and deeds he had adopted. The positive is that he adopts correct beliefs and deeds in their place. For the first, the word "istighfar" (seeking forgiveness) is used, the concept of which is that the servant supplicates to his Lord that He overflows his sins and covers them with His forgiveness and grace. For the second, the word "tawbah" (repentance) is used, which means turning back. That is, the servant turns back towards that straight path of life which God has shown him and which leads him to God. The foundation of the first of these is on fear, and of the second on love; then, consciousness and feeling are their essential parts. As long as all these elements are not gathered, merely repeating "tawbah, tawbah" or "astaghfirullah" with the tongue does not bring into existence that repentance which attains the rank of acceptance with God. "He will send the sky upon you in abundance and increase you in strength to your strength"—the abundant falling of rain is an expression of increase in sustenance and grace. And "and increase you in strength to your strength" refers to the increase in political power and grandeur. These have been described as the blessings of collective repentance. The meaning is: do not think that the rain from the sky is showered by your deities and victory in the battlefield is granted to you by them, because of which, if you leave them, you will remain deprived of sustenance and your political collective strength will also be shattered. These thoughts of yours are based entirely on illusion. The sovereignty over the heavens and the earth belongs only to God. For this reason, if any nation turns to Allah and holds His rope firmly, Allah Almighty opens for them the doors of the blessings of both the heavens and the earth, and their rule is established over the four corners of the world. Accept the call of seeking forgiveness and repentance which our Messenger is giving you with obedience; do not turn away from it criminally. Although here, as we indicated, the blessings of collective repentance have been described, it does not mean that individual repentance is devoid of these blessings. Whichever servant turns away from a life of sin and turns to Allah, Allah Almighty provides for him with pure sustenance and grants him the everlasting kingdom of peace and tranquility.

They said, "O Hud, you have not brought us any clear proof, and we are not ones to leave our gods on your say-so, and we are not believers in you."

The Demand of the Nation for a Miracle: By "bayyinah" (clear proof) here is meant some open, physical miracle. The meaning is: how can we accept such a great claim of yours—that you have come to us as the Messenger of God—and such a great demand—that we leave our deities—merely on your saying so? We could accept this claim and demand only in the event when you showed some open miracle. But when you have not brought any such miracle, we are neither prepared to leave our deities merely on your saying, nor are we prepared to confirm your claim that you are the Messenger of God.

54–55. We only say that some of our gods have possessed you with evil." He said, "Indeed, I call Allah to witness, and witness yourselves, that I am free of whatever you associate [with Him] other than Him. So plot against me all together; then do not give me respite."

The Honor of Hud (peace be upon him) for the Truth: "I'tara" means reaching and befalling—that is: accepting this claim and demand of yours is far off. Our saying is only that you have been struck by one of our deities, under the effect of which you have become mad and have started saying such wild things. As soon as he heard this word of theirs, Hud's honor for monotheism surged. From the tone of speech, it appears that he immediately cut off their word and replied with full passion: I call Allah to witness, and you too bear witness, that I am free of whatever you associate besides Him. "So plot against me all together; then do not give me respite"—along with the announcement of exoneration, this is a challenge: if your assumption is that these deities of yours can cause me some harm, then you and all these deities of yours together make whatever plot you want to make against me, and then do not give me any respite. That is, try even the last arrows of your quiver so that no desire remains in your heart.

Indeed, I have relied upon Allah, my Lord and your Lord. There is no creature but that He holds its forelock. Indeed, my Lord is on a path straight.

The Shield of the Believer—Reliance (Tawakkul): This is that shield of the believer and monotheist on the strength of which Hud gave the aforementioned challenge. He said: my reliance is upon Allah, Who is my Lord as well as yours. There is no possibility that anyone can cause any harm without His permission. "There is no creature but that He holds its forelock"—there is no living creature that is out of His grasp of power. The forelock of all is in His hand. He can turn whom He wills wheresoever He wills by holding their forelock, and can stop them where He wills. "Indeed, my Lord is on a path straight"—and do not think that to reach my Lord, I need to pass through many crooked paths and, according to your belief, need many mediums and means. Rather, my intellect and my nature have a direct connection with Him. To reach Him, it is sufficient that I turn to Him with a sincere heart; if I turn, He is before me on a completely straight path. It should be remembered here that the belief of the polytheists was that the medium to reach God was only their idols, which is why without their worship, no one could find God. They used to present their very belief thus: "We do not worship them except that they may bring us closer to Allah in position." On the basis of this very belief of theirs, the Quran has stated their crime to be that they prevent people from the straight path to God and want to make this straight path crooked ("Who avert [people] from the way of Allah and seek to make it deviant"). Hud has refuted this very false belief, and if you ponder, the reality will come forward that as long as there is no such concept of proximity and connection with God within a person as exists within a true monotheist, he cannot have that reliance on God that he can challenge the entire creation like Hud.

But if you turn away, then I have already conveyed to you that with which I was sent to you. And my Lord will give succession to a people other than you, and you will not harm Him at all. Indeed, my Lord is, over all things, Guardian.

The Final Warning of Hud: This is the final warning from Hud: if you have decided to persist in this very turning away, then all the responsibility of the results is upon your own head, because the message that was sent with me from Allah Almighty, I have clearly conveyed to you. Now the next stage is that my Lord will cut off your roots. "And my Lord will give succession to a people other than you"—and will establish some other nation in your place. "and you will not harm Him at all"—and do not think that by your destruction, God's world will become desolate. Your annihilation is not harmful to Him or His world in the least; He is the Guardian and Watcher over everything. He removes what becomes corrupt and brings another new thing in its place. It is not that He made this world once and then sat disconnected from it, rather He is constantly watching over it. If any nation exceeds its limits, He also corrects it after giving respite up to a certain limit.

And when Our command came, We saved Hud and those who believed with him, by mercy from Us; and We saved them from a harsh punishment.

The Nature of the Punishment of 'Aad: By "amr" (command) here is meant that punishment which manifested under the Divine command. What was the nature of this punishment? There is no detail of it here. Our teacher, Maulana Farahi, has discussed its nature in detail in the Tafseer of Surah Adh-Dhariyat. Maulana raised the introduction of this discussion thus:

"Whoever ponders over the details of the destruction of the nation of 'Aad described in the Holy Quran, the reality cannot remain hidden from him that they were destroyed through a fierce wind with which those clouds of winter were also present which always appear with thunder and lightning. In the Quran, wherever their destruction is mentioned, wind has been mentioned along with clouds devoid of rain and thunderbolts (sa'iqah). In Surah Al-Ahqaf, it says: 'Then, when they saw it as a cloud approaching their valleys, they said, "This is a cloud bringing us rain!" Rather, it is that for which you were impatient: a wind, within it a painful punishment, destroying everything by command of its Lord.' Obviously, all these characteristics are of the fierce wind of the season. In this era, in Arabia, the wind Shamal blows in the form of Sarsar, and a general wretchedness and destruction of famine and dryness spreads everywhere. In Surah Al-Qamar, allusion has been made to this very thing: 'Indeed, We sent upon them a shrieking wind on a day of continuous misfortune.' Similarly, in Surah Fussilat: 'So We sent upon them a shrieking wind in days of misfortune.' These storms of wind Sarsar, as we have submitted, appear in winter in Arabia, and this very season is considered among them as a time of misfortune and starvation." (Tafseer of Surah Adh-Dhariyat, Farahi, Chapter 17).

Allah's Favor upon the Believers: When this punishment descended, Allah Almighty, according to His sunnah, saved His Messenger Hud and his believing companions out of His special mercy ("rahmah") and destroyed all the rest. From "by mercy from Us", the greatness and majesty of this mercy is manifest, because for such a punishment that came over the entire region, to hide a few specific people under the wings of His mercy and save them is a grand miracle of Divine favor. "and We saved them from a harsh punishment"—that is, do not think that We delivered them from some ordinary calamity, rather We delivered them from an extremely severe and harsh punishment. From this, the dreadfulness of the punishment is also expressed, and also that grand mercy which manifested for Hud and his companions. Commentators have generally interpreted this as the punishment of the Hereafter, but the specific aspect we have clarified holds an extremely clear suitability to the context.

Commentary of Verses 59–60: "And that was 'Aad, who rejected the signs of their Lord and disobeyed His messengers and followed the command of every obstinate tyrant. And they were followed in this world with a curse and [as well] on the Day of Resurrection. Unquestionably, 'Aad disbelieved in their Lord; so away with 'Aad, the people of Hud."

Warning to the Quraish: The pronoun "tilka" (that) is to draw the attention of the addressees—namely, the Quraish—to this story, that: learn a lesson and take heed from their end; this is not the story of anyone else, it is the event of your own land. "who rejected the signs of their Lord"—they too, like you, deliberately rejected the signs of their Lord, as is explicitly stated in another place. "and disobeyed His messengers"—and disobeyed His Messengers. Here it should be kept in mind that although apparently they had disobeyed only one Messenger—namely, Hud (peace be upon him)—the plural word "messengers" (rusul) has been used. The reason for this is that the consideration is of the original cause of the action, not of the specific action. The cause on the basis of which they denied Hud, in the presence of its existence, their dealing with any Messenger could not be different from this. "and followed the command of every obstinate tyrant"—that is, they disobeyed the Messengers and obeyed the rebellious and the obstinate. The end of this wretched path was: "And they were followed in this world with a curse and on the Day of Resurrection"—a curse was set behind them in this world and in the Hereafter too this curse will remain set behind them. This reality should also be kept in view here: in this world, there are only two paths for man—one of the Merciful (Rahman), the second of Satan; and the following of one of them is inevitable for him. If he does not adopt the path of the Merciful, he will inevitably follow Satan. There is no other middle path. At the end, the Quraish were warned again: listen with open ears that 'Aad disbelieved in their Lord. "so away with 'Aad, the people of Hud"—know that upon the nation of 'Aad is the strike of God.

And to Thamud [We sent] their brother Salih. He said, "O my people, worship Allah; you have no deity other than Him. He has produced you from the earth and settled you in it, so ask forgiveness of Him and then repent to Him. Indeed, my Lord is near and responsive."

After 'Aad, among the ancient nations of Arabia, Thamud attained great fame in terms of their cultural and architectural progress. Under Surah Al-A'raf, verse 74, the mention of their architectural progress has passed. To them, Allah Almighty sent Salih as a Messenger, who was their own brother, so that national estrangement does not become a cause of any wildness and alienation. Along with the call to monotheism, he also drew the attention of his nation to this reality: it is God Who created you from the earth and put you in its maintenance and progress. "He has produced you from the earth and settled you in it"—"Ista'marahu fil-makan" means he put him in its reform and construction. The meaning is: the progress you have been blessed with in construction and civilization is also God's gift; only by virtue of His bestowed capabilities have you become capable of performing these feats, so do not become corrupters on the earth being proud of these feats, rather seek forgiveness for your sins and turn to Him with a sincere heart. "Indeed, my Lord is near and responsive"—my Lord is near and also the Acceptor of prayers and supplications. That is, to find Him, it is sufficient that you turn to Him with sincerity. There is absolutely no need for those intercessors and partners whom you make a necessary condition to attain His proximity.

They said, "O Salih, you were among us one hoped for before this. Do you forbid us to worship what our fathers worshipped? And indeed we are, about that to which you invite us, in disquieting doubt."

By "marjuwwan" (one hoped for) is meant that person whose upbringing and start has been so good that he becomes the center of hopes and desires of his nation and tribe, and people start associating great hopes with him in the future. Since the Prophets (peace be upon them) were the best fruit of the tree of nature, even before being elevated to the rank of prophethood, they were highly distinguished in their entire nation in terms of their high capabilities and their pure character and attributes. The demand of this attribute of theirs was that when they came forward with the claim of prophethood, people should have pondered over their word with seriousness, but it is a strange thing that the dealing of the nations of the Prophets has generally been contrary to this. The very person whom they believed to be truthful and trustworthy throughout his life, as soon as he expressed his prophethood, they started calling him a liar and fabricator and went after his life. Only a few people emerged who were granted the ability to ponder over the matter with seriousness.

The Objections of the Nation to Salih: The nation of Salih also had a great good opinion of him beforehand, but when he came forward as a Prophet, gave the call to monotheism, and forbade shirk, their entire good opinion vanished. They said: we had great hopes associated with you beforehand—that the name of the fathers would be brightened through you and there would be an increase in the honor and majesty of the ancestral religion; but you turned out to be such that you rose to forbid us from the worship of those deities whom our fathers worshipped. Brother, the true word is that due to this call which you are giving us, we have fallen into severe doubt and confusion. In "Do you forbid us to worship" (Atanhana an na'buda), the preposition "an" has been omitted. Originally it is "an an na'buda". By "about that to which you invite us" is meant Salih's call to monotheism. With the attribute of "mureeb" (disquieting) along with doubt, this addition has been made to the theme: your this call has given a great blow to our hopes. What expectations we were holding, and what mischief you have risen with! "Araba" in lexicography means "aza'ajahu wa aqlaqahu", i.e., he put him in anxiety and confusion.

He said, "O my people, have you considered: if I should be upon a clear proof from my Lord and He has given me mercy from Him, who would protect me from Allah if I disobeyed Him? So you would not increase me except in loss."

Salih's Response: "Bayyinah" and "rahmah" have been discussed under verses 17 and 28. The meaning is: I, who am giving the call, it is the voice of my nature and moreover the teaching of the divine revelation which I received directly; so now, if I deviate from this path and adopt some other path, who will save me from God's grip? "So you would not increase me except in loss"—that is, if I leave this path and engage in fulfilling your expectations, you will only increase me in wretchedness and disappointment, you will not be able to help me at all against God.

Commentary of Verses 64–65: "And O my people, this is the she-camel of Allah—[she is] to you a sign. So let her feed upon Allah's earth and do not touch her with harm, lest there take you a pending punishment." But they hamstrung her, so he said, "Enjoy yourselves in your homes for three days. That is a promise not to be denied."

The Miracle of the She-Camel: This exact theme, in almost these very words, has passed in Surah Al-A'raf, verse 73. There, we have explained all its important parts and have also clarified the occasion and purpose of the manifestation of the she-camel's sign.

So when Our command came, We saved Salih and those who believed with him, by mercy from Us, and [saved them] from the disgrace of that Day. Indeed, it is your Lord who is the Powerful, the Exalted in Might.

Warning to the Quraish, Consolation to the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): By "amr" (command) is meant that punishment which came upon Thamud by the command of Allah Almighty. Declaring the punishment by the word "amr" holds this eloquence: as soon as the command was issued, the punishment immediately arrived. It is as if the punishment was implicit within the command itself. "We saved Salih and those who believed with him, by mercy from Us"—that is, We saved Salih and those who believed with him out of Our special grace. Otherwise, this calamity was so absolute and all-encompassing that except for Allah's mercy, nothing else could have been a savior from it. "And from the disgrace of that Day"—meaning "and We saved them from the disgrace of that Day", just as above in verse 58: "And We saved them from a harsh punishment". There, the verb has been made apparent. Here, due to the presence of the context, it has been omitted. The meaning is: do not think that We saved them from some ordinary calamity, rather We saved them from the disgrace of that day, the disgrace of which is well-known to the common and elite. This matter should also be kept in mind here: when punishment has come upon any nation as a result of denial of the Messenger, it has come upon those after complete reminder, preaching, and complete establishment of the proof, who out of their pride were not prepared to listen to and understand any word at all. For this reason, its prominent aspect has also been that it did not merely crush them, but also disgraced and humiliated them in the highest degree. "Indeed, it is your Lord who is the Powerful, the Exalted in Might"—this is a message of peace and consolation for the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): the real Owner of power and honor is only your Lord. If any share of it is received by anyone, it is received only by His grace, so rest assured; just as He disgraced and crushed the enemies of Salih, He will also disgrace your enemies one day, and no one will be able to hold His hand.

Commentary of Verses 67 to 68: "And the shriek seized those who had wronged, and they became within their homes [corpses] fallen prone. As if they had never prospered therein. Unquestionably, Thamud disbelieved in their Lord; so away with Thamud."

The Nature of the Punishment upon the Nation of Thamud: "Sayhah" means a shriek or shout. By this is meant that punishment which came upon the nation of Thamud. Since the consideration was of the meaning and not of the literal word, the verb used for it was masculine. Upon this nation, Allah Almighty sent a winter shrieking wind and a thunderclap, hail, and earthquake. "and they became within their homes fallen prone, as if they had never prospered therein"—the result was that they remained lying flat on their faces in their homes, and became without name and trace as if they had never lived in them. "so away with Thamud" is a sentence of expressing hatred and curse. Its explanation has passed above.

And certainly did Our messengers come to Abraham with good tidings; they said, "Peace." He said, "Peace," and did not delay in bringing a roasted calf.

The Story of Lot (peace be upon him) and the Nation of Lot: Now ahead comes the story of Lot and his nation. This verse has been entered here as an introduction to this story, because those angels who had brought the punishment for the nation of Lot had also brought the glad tidings of a son and grandson for Abraham. By "messengers" (rusul) here is meant those very angels. By "good tidings" (bushra) is meant, as is explicitly stated ahead in verse 71, the birth of Isaac and then from him Jacob.

Abraham's Hospitality for the Angels: "they said, 'Peace.' He said, 'Peace.'"—that is, they greeted Abraham with peace like pious and decent people. And Abraham also replied to their greeting of peace. And since they were in human form, Abraham immediately engaged in preparing hospitality for them. "and did not delay in bringing a roasted calf"—and not much time passed before he presented a roasted calf before them. From this, the generosity and guest-friendship of Abraham can be estimated—that a few men come to the door, with whom there is no prior acquaintance, but he without delay gets a calf from the herd slaughtered for them and presents its roasted meat before them. From the word "calf" (ijl), it is not necessarily implied that a whole roasted calf was presented, but rather this is a well-known style of language: sometimes the whole is spoken and the part is meant by it, because of which its meaning can also be that he presented the roasted meat of a calf before them. This matter should be kept in mind here: in the nomadic period of life, this hospitality was extremely generous—that for the entertainment of three men, Abraham got a calf of the herd slaughtered. To highlight this generosity of theirs, the calf was mentioned instead of meat.

But when he saw their hands not reaching for it, he mistrusted them and felt from them apprehension. They said, "Fear not. Indeed, we have been sent to the people of Lot."

Abraham's Anxiety and the Angels' Reassurance: Until now, Abraham was under the assumption that they were some honorable and pious men of some nearby area, but when they did not extend their hands towards the hospitality prepared with such labor and enthusiasm, he naturally felt some estrangement and feared in his heart: what is the matter? They did not accept his hospitality. Could it be that he considered them human and they were angels? And if they are angels, they must inevitably have set out on some great mission, because the manifestation of angels occurs only for some great mission. On this occasion, the thought of the nation of Lot must have naturally come to his mind, which was nearby, in opposition to the struggle of Lot (peace be upon him), having entered that stage of their transgression after which God's punishment inevitably arrives. The angels, sensing that Abraham had fallen into anxiety, reassured him: do not fall into any anxiety; we have been sent to the nation of Lot, and from the context, it is known that on this very occasion, they made him hear the glad tidings of a son.

71–72. And his Wife was standing, and she laughed. Then We gave her good tidings of Isaac and after Isaac, Jacob. She said, "Woe to me! Shall I give birth while I am an old woman and this, my husband, is an old man? Indeed, this is an amazing thing!"

The Astonishment and Joy of Sarah: The story had initiated with: "did Our messengers come to Abraham with good tidings". But as soon as he saw the guests, Abraham immediately engaged in the preparation of their hospitality. For this reason, the turn to hear the glad tidings came now, and at this time, it was revealed to them that those whom they considered human were not human but angels. At the time when the angels gave the glad tidings, Abraham's wife Sarah was also standing by the door. "and she laughed"—that is, hearing this glad tidings, she laughed. This laughter was the manifestation of various emotions of astonishment, wonder, and joy. The expression of this astonishment which she made in words, its reference is coming ahead. The mercy of Allah Almighty surged further upon this joyful manifestation of her astonishment. Accordingly, addressing her directly, she was given the glad tidings of not only a son but, with name specified, of a son and further of a grandson: "Then We gave her good tidings of Isaac and after Isaac, Jacob." In "Then We gave her good tidings", the special favors and completion of joy that are maintained are beyond description, and the glad tidings of a grandson along with the son gave, as it were, the reassurance that the son will live, achieve a good age, and from his loin, a famous grandson will also be born. "She said, 'Woe to me!'"—this is the detail of Sarah's expression of astonishment, to which reference was made above by the word "and she laughed". She spoke in a purely feminine way: Woe is me! Shall I now give birth to a child when I have become a barren old woman and this husband of mine has also become an old man? This would be an extremely amazing thing! Apparently, this is an expression of astonishment, but the inner happiness that is shining through every single word of it is not in need of explanation. A special aspect of Sarah's expression of astonishment is that by mentioning the apparent obstacles in the way of the manifestation of this glad tidings, she may obtain reassurance that despite them, it will be fulfilled.

They said, "Are you amazed at the decree of Allah? May the mercy of Allah and His blessings be upon you, people of the house. Indeed, He is Praiseworthy and Honorable."

The Reassurance of the Angels: From the word "Are you amazed" (Ata'jabeena), it is clearly manifest that the angels took Sarah's laughter and her sentence in its completely correct meaning—namely, the concept of expressing astonishment—and in a highly respectful and honorable tone, they drew Sarah's attention: O household of the Prophet! Where is the room for astonishment at any work of God and His intention? Moreover, upon you are His special favors and His special mercies and blessings; He is highly Praiseworthy and highly Magnificent. In "May the mercy of Allah and His blessings be upon you, people of the house" (alaykum ahl-al-bayt), the use of the masculine plural pronoun "alaykum" is an example of the polite mode of address in the Arabic language. In this mode of addressing women, the state of veil-keeping and respect is beyond expression. Extremely clear and subtle examples of this are present in the Holy Quran and Arabic speech. In Surah Al-Ahzab, it says: "Allah intends only to remove from you the impure, O people of the [Prophet's] household, and to purify you with [extensive] purification." A verse of Imru' al-Qais is also worthy of mention: "If the dwellers of the house were in it as we once knew, I would have found a place of rest with them and lodging."

And when the fright had gone from Abraham and the good tidings had reached him, he began to argue with Us concerning the people of Lot.

Abraham's Intercession for the Nation of Lot: "Yujadiluna" means "sara yujadiluna" (he began to argue with us); multiple examples of the omission of the defective verb before the aorist verb have passed above. In verse 38, "Wa yasna'ul-fulka" is also on this style. The word "mujadalah" (arguing) here is in the sense of "good arguing" (mujadalah-e-hasan)—that is, with good manners, love, and trust, trying to make someone accept one's word with insistence and pleading. As long as the original plan of the angels was not clear to Abraham, he remained anxious and worried, but when he received the glad tidings of a son and became reassured regarding his own household, he became concerned for the nation of Lot and engaged in interceding for them with Allah Almighty, and tried every device to make his word accepted. We translate the details of this arguing from the Torah so that some estimate can be made of what kind of arguing is liked in the eyes of Allah Almighty:

"And Abraham stood yet before the Lord. And Abraham drew near, and said, Wilt thou also destroy the righteous with the wicked? Peradventure there be fifty righteous within the city: wilt thou also destroy and not spare the place for the fifty righteous that are therein? That be far from thee to do after this manner, to slay the righteous with the wicked: and that the righteous should be as the wicked, that be far from thee: Shall not the Judge of all the earth do right? And the Lord said, If I find in Sodom fifty righteous within the city, then I will spare all the place for their sakes. And Abraham answered and said, Behold now, I have taken upon me to speak unto the Lord, which am but dust and ashes: Peradventure there shall lack five of the fifty righteous: wilt thou destroy all the city for lack of five? And he said, If I find there forty and five, I will not destroy it. And he spake unto him yet again, and said, Peradventure there shall be forty found there. And he said, I will not do it for forty's sake. And he said unto him, Oh let not the Lord be angry, and I will speak: Peradventure there shall thirty be found there. And he said, I will not do it, if I find thirty there. And he said, Behold now, I have taken upon me to speak unto the Lord: Peradventure there shall be twenty found there. And he said, I will not destroy it for twenty's sake. And he said, Oh let not the Lord be angry, and I will speak yet but this once: Peradventure ten shall be found there. And he said, I will not destroy it for ten's sake." (Genesis, Chapter 18: 22–32).

This arguing of Abraham with his Lord, which enfolds within itself the aspects of love, trust, pampering, and pain and sympathy, the tongue of the pen is unable to express and explain. Accordingly, Allah Almighty praised Abraham for this.

Indeed, Abraham was forbearing, grieving, and penitent. "Awwah" is an intensive form (mubalaghah) of "ah" (sighing), the literal concept of which is: one who sighs frequently—that is, highly sympathetic, highly sorrowful, and possessor of pain. When Abraham pours out his heart in this way for the intercession of a wretched nation like the nation of Lot, what room for doubt remains in his forbearance and sympathy? And what greater example of perfect penitence (inabat) can there be than this—that apparently he knows that now the measure of the nation of Lot is full, but he is constantly active in prayer and supplication.

O Abraham, dismiss this! Indeed, there has come the command of your Lord, and indeed, there will reach them a punishment irreversible.

The Response to Abraham: Allah Almighty praised this sympathy, pain, prayer, and supplication of Abraham, but at the same time, He also informed him that now the time for prayer and intercession has passed. "Indeed, there has come the command of your Lord"—now the command of your Lord, namely the command of punishment, has come. And an irreversible punishment is about to reach them. From "a punishment irreversible", firstly, there is an allusion to the reality that this punishment is not of any warning nature, such that it merely warns and passes, but rather it is a decisive punishment which will break their back. Secondly, there is also an allusion in this to the fact that this punishment cannot be averted by anyone's averting, because the power to oppose the command of God is not in anyone.

And when Our messengers came to Lot, he was distressed for them and felt for them great discomfort and said, "This is a difficult day."

Lot's Anxiety at the Arrival of the Angels: After conveying the glad tidings to Abraham and informing him of the end of the nation of Lot, these angels reached Lot. When Lot saw that several handsome young men were at the door, he became highly distressed and tight of heart. In his heart, he said: today will be an extremely difficult day. The reason for this distress is clear: when the nation was involved in that moral corruption in which Lot's nation was involved, the arrival of such handsome new-comers at the door was, as it were, equivalent to inviting all the ruffians of the city.

And his people came rushing to him, and before they had been doing evil deeds. He said, "O my people, these are my daughters; they are purer for you. So fear Allah and do not disgrace me concerning my guests. Is there not among you a man of reason?"

Lot's Cry: Accordingly, his fear proved entirely correct. When the wicked saw that some handsome boys had come to Lot's door, they came rushing. When Lot saw their behavior, he said: "O my people, these are my daughters; they are purer for you. So fear Allah and do not disgrace me concerning my guests." This was not an offer, but rather, to awaken and shake the conscience of his nation, it was, as it were, Lot's final restless cry—that they should think: a servant of Allah is this one, who is so sensitive in the matter of the honor of his guests that he is prepared to sacrifice his dearest thing for it, and we are such that, becoming blind, we have fallen upon his guests. Similarly, he also reminded them of the fear of Allah, and at the end, by saying "Is there not among you a man of reason?" he completely established the proof upon them. Because if there had been even a grain's worth of feeling of honor and support of truth in anyone, he must have moved after this sentence; but when even after this no conscience awoke, its clear meaning is that humanity and decency did not remain in anyone at all.

They said, "You have already known that we have not in your daughters any right, and indeed, you know what we want."

The Obstinacy of the Nation of Lot: They said in reply: what is the benefit of increasing and complicating the talk, you know well that we have no right to lay hands on your daughters. What we want is not hidden from you. The meaning is: why do you stand as an obstacle in this desire of ours without cause? Let us do what we want.

He said, "If only I had against you some power or could take refuge in a strong support."

Lot's Final Attempt to Shake the Conscience of the Nation: This sentence came out of Lot's tongue in extreme anxiety and after being disappointed in the highest degree by the attitude of the ruffians. He said: if only I had either my own power and group to deal with you, or there was some influential person with a group that I could take refuge with him and he could save me from your high-handedness. This final sentence was also spoken by Lot to shake the conscience of the nation. Here it should be remembered that in Arabia, to give refuge to an afflicted and oppressed person when he was a seeker of refuge was considered a matter of great honor, and similarly, not to give refuge to a seeker of refuge was a proof of extreme baseness. Lot, by speaking this sentence, established the final proof as well. After this proof was established, the angels unveiled the reality and spoke (see the next verse).

They said, "O Lot, indeed we are messengers of your Lord; they will never reach you. So travel with your family during a portion of the night and let not any among you look back—except your wife; indeed, she will be struck by that which strikes them. Indeed, their appointment is for the morning. Is not the morning near?"

They spoke: O Lot, do not be worried, we are not boys, as these satans have assumed, rather we are the envoys of your Lord. They can never reach you; so do thus: travel in the night with your family from this town, and let none of you look back. Yes, your wife is excepted from your family. Upon her too is to come that calamity which has been destined for the entire nation. Their appointed time is the time of morning. Is not the morning near?

82–83. So when Our command came, We made the highest part [of the city] its lowest and rained upon them stones of layered hard clay, marked from your Lord. And they are not from the unjust far.

That is, when Our command arrived, We sent upon them such a fierce wind that it turned their houses completely upside down. "and rained upon them stones of layered hard clay"—"Sijjeel" is the arabized form of "sang-e-gil" (baked clay/stone of clay). That is, We showered upon their town layer upon layer of baked clay stones. "marked from your Lord"—they were marked with your Lord, i.e., in God's knowledge and His power, they were already destined and appointed. It is as if they were piled and marked beforehand that these piles are to be rained upon the town of the nation of Lot. "And they are not from the unjust far"—and these baked clay stones were not far from these unjust people that it would take some time to bring them from there, rather from right there under their feet, Our sent fierce wind ("hasib") picked them up and rained them upon their heads.

By "Unjust" (Zalimun) is Meant the Quraish: "And they are not from the unjust far"—in the interpretation of this sentence, there is also room to understand the referent of "hiya" (they/it) to be the town of Lot, and by "zalimun" (unjust) is meant the Quraish. The meaning is: the town of Lot is not far from the Quraish; they pass by this very town in their journey to Syria. If they possess the eye of learning a lesson, let them learn a lesson from it. Ahead in verse 89, Shuaib said addressing his nation: "And the people of Lot are not far from you."

The Nature of the Punishment of the Nation of Lot: The summary of Maulana Farahi's research regarding the punishment of the nation of Lot is this: "Upon the nation of Lot, Allah Almighty sent a dust-raising wind which, becoming severe, eventually became 'hasib' (a fierce wind raining pebbles and stones). From this, firstly, a rain of pebbles and stones fell upon them. Then it took such intensity that under its force, their houses were also overturned. Accordingly, pointing to them, it says: 'And of them were those upon whom We sent a storm of stones.' Also it says: 'So We made its highest part its lowest and rained upon them stones of layered hard clay.' That is, such fierce winds blew that their houses and roofs all became equal to the ground, and from above, pebbles and sand covered them." (Tafseer of Surah Adh-Dhariyat, Farahi, Chapter 15).

And to Madyan [We sent] their brother Shuaib. He said, "O my people, worship Allah; you have no deity other than Him. And do not decrease from the measure and the scale. Indeed, I see you in prosperity, but indeed, I fear for you the punishment of an all-encompassing Day."

The Nature of the Corruption of the Nation of Shuaib: Clarification of this matter has passed above—that in sending a brother of theirs as a Messenger to every nation, what is the wisdom from the perspective of the establishment of the proof? And this reality has also been well clarified—that monotheism holds the position of the center of gravity and pivotal point in the call of all Prophets. Deviation from this opens the doors to all deviations, and return to this is the means to the guidance of the straight path. Accordingly, from this very point, Shuaib also initiated his call, and then turned his attention to the reform of that evil of his nation which had not only become common in the entire nation but had also begun to be considered as a professional skill and virtue, and right and proper, instead of an evil. His nation—the people of Madyan—was a trading nation. For this reason, their corruption of temperament erupted most in this field, and they made decreasing in measure and scale their professional skill. One after another, experts in this art began to be born among them, and no feeling remained in anyone that this is not the path of progress and success, but rather the path of corruption on the earth. Shuaib invited them to this reform, saying: O people, do not decrease in measure and scale; currently I see you in prosperity and abundance, and I fear for you the punishment of a day that will completely encompass you. The meaning is: currently the prosperity and abundance you have been blessed with naturally demands that you should be highly grateful to your Lord and highly obedient to His law of justice and equity. Allah Almighty blesses any nation with prosperity and abundance only to see whether that nation becomes grateful to God or adopts the path of ingratitude and disobedience. If it adopts this second path, then this very abundance and prosperity becomes the preface to a punishment which encompasses them in such a way that no one can escape from its encompassment. I see the signs of this sudden death within your current fatness, so take care of yourselves as soon as possible.

And O my people, give full measure and weight in justice and do not deprive people of their things and do not commit abuse on the earth, spreading corruption.

Shuaib's Call to Reform: "Qist" means justice and equity—that is, whether it is measure or weight, keep full justice and equity in view in both. "and do not deprive people of their things"—and do not do any injustice and decrease in the measure and weight of people. "and do not commit abuse on the earth, spreading corruption"—that is, when you progress on the earth, progress as bearers and standard-bearers of good, reform, justice, and equity. Do not progress as those who shatter its justice and equity and spread corruption in it. To such a progress, the Creator and Master of this earth gives respite only up to a certain limit. As soon as this respite passes, He cleanses His earth of such corrupters.

What remains [lawful] from Allah is best for you, if you should be believers. And I am not over you a guardian.

By "Baqiyyatullah" (what remains from Allah) is meant lawful profit. If a trader respects exact justice in measure and weight, protects himself from lie, deceit, mixing, and other such tactics, and does not adopt wrong paths in imitation of others, then even if he cannot plunder as much profit as others who have removed the distinction between lawful and prohibited have plundered, what he has acquired holds the status of "Baqiyyatullah" and, even if small, is thousands of times better than the abundance of others, because in it lies blessing in both the world and the Hereafter. Contrary to this, the profit acquired through dishonesty will be devoid of blessing in the world and a burning fire in the Hereafter. "And I am not over you a guardian"—that is, I have explained good and bad to you well, and this alone was my responsibility. If you accept, in it lies your own benefit. If you do not accept, you alone will see its bad end; no harm will reach me from it. I am only a warner and bringer of glad tidings, I have not been appointed as a watcher and warden over you that the accounting of your not believing should be with me.

They said, "O Shuaib, does your prayer command you that we should leave what our fathers worship or that we should not do with our wealth what we will? Indeed, you are the forbearing, the discerning!"

The Irony of the Nation against Shuaib: This entire sentence is in an ironical style. They said: does your prayer teach you only this—that we should leave those deities whom our fathers worshipped, and should not dispose of our wealth according to our own discretion? From these words of yours, it appears that in your eyes, all the past and present were foolish and misguided, and only you have remained wise, seeing, and guided. The meaning is: you used to pray etc., so from that, the idea was that through you, the name of the fathers would be brightened and some paths of success would open for the nation, but your this prayer turned out to be very strange—that it wants to fold up the entire carpet of the past and the present. This matter should be kept in mind here: since the corrupters feared that the people who are naturally good would be influenced by the good and pure life of Shuaib, they made his very virtues the target of their irony, as if the root of all corruption was those very virtues.

He said, "O my people, have you considered: if I should be upon a clear proof from my Lord and He has provided me from Himself with a good provision? And I do not intend to differ from you in that which I forbid you; I only intend reform as much as I am able. And my success is not but through Allah. Upon Him I have relied, and to Him I turn."

The Meaning of "Bayyinah" and "Good Provision" (Rizq Hasan): The research on the word "bayyinah" has passed above under verse 63. By this is meant that conviction and belief which Allah Almighty has deposited within every sound nature and which guides man towards the straight path, on the condition that man has not distorted his nature by being influenced by the effects of a wrong environment. "and He has provided me from Himself with a good provision"—by "good provision" (rizq hasan) here is expressed the very thing which in the aforementioned verse 63 was expressed by "rahmah" (mercy), namely divine revelation. The reason for expressing divine revelation by the term "good provision" is that just as material provision is necessary for the survival of man's material life, similarly, spiritual life is also dependent on spiritual provision. Jesus expressed this very reality thus: "Man shall not live by bread alone, but by every word that proceedeth out of the mouth of God."

In the first sentence, the response to the condition is omitted. If this omission is opened in the light of verse 63, which is a verse of exactly this theme, then the entire statement would be: tell me, if I am upon a clear proof from my Lord, and then He has also blessed me from Himself with a good provision, then if after this too I deviate from His path, who will save me from God's wrath? The meaning is: this which I am telling you, you are attributing it to affectation and fabrication and making me the target of irony and contempt, but what can I do? This was the voice of my nature beforehand, and then confirming and proving it, my Lord also sent down revelation upon me; so if I do not present this before you, what else should I present? "And I do not intend to differ from you in that which I forbid you"—that is, do not harbor this suspicion that I, who am forbidding you from dishonesty in measure and weight, want to forbid you from it so that I may do this work myself and in this way, excluding you from the market, may occupy the market myself. It is possible that Shuaib (peace be upon him) used to do some small trade himself for his livelihood. From this thing, the wicked took advantage and raised this rumor: this person who is preaching honesty in measure and weight with such vehemence, his purpose in this is only that we should become honest and he may do as he wills and occupy the entire market. Shuaib tried to remove their suspicion: that which I am forbidding you, I am not forbidding so that I may occupy it myself. "I only intend reform as much as I am able"—I only want reform as far as my power goes. "And my success is not but through Allah"—and in this struggle, success and guidance will be achieved only by the favor of Allah. Upon Him I have relied, and to Him I turn.

And O my people, let not your dissension from me cause you to be struck by the like of what struck the people of Noah or the people of Hud or the people of Salih. And the people of Lot are not from you far.

Shuaib's Warning: "Shiqaq" means obstinacy and opposition. The meaning is: do not cut off your own nose to spite your face. The path you have adopted in my opposition, let it not become the cause for you that some punishment of this kind descends upon you as descended upon the nation of Noah, or the nation of Hud, or the nation of Salih before this, and the nation of Lot is not far from you either. This matter should be kept in mind here: the nation of Lot was close to the nation of Shuaib in terms of both time and their dwelling place. The meaning of Shuaib's statement is: do not think that I am only narrating to you the stories of the distant past, but rather the testimony of your near past and your nearby area is also that your path is inviting the punishment of God.

And ask forgiveness of your Lord and then repent to Him. Indeed, my Lord is Merciful and Loving.

That is, if you want welfare, listen to my word and seek forgiveness of your Lord for your sins and turn to Him. By turning (tawbah) is meant: abstaining from your current path, adopt that path which is God's beloved path and towards which I am inviting you. This reality should remain in view: repentance has two fundamental pillars—one "istighfar" (seeking forgiveness), the second "islaah" (reform/rectification); that is, a person should seek forgiveness of his Lord for his crimes, and also give practical proof of the reform of his attitude by adopting the correct path. Without this, no repentance is accepted in the sight of Allah Almighty.

"Indeed, my Lord is Merciful and Loving"—this is the reference to Allah Almighty's attributes of mercy and love. The objective of this is encouragement and invitation to seeking forgiveness and repentance, and in an extremely subtle way, the glad tidings of the acceptance of repentance. The target is: no matter how grave your crimes may be, when you turn to Him with a sincere heart, He will not reject you, but rather will forgive you and bless you with His mercies; He is highly Kind and Loving.

They said, "O Shuaib, we do not understand much of what you say, and indeed, we see you among us as weak. And if not for your family, we would have stoned you; and you are not against us noble."

The Threat to Stone Shuaib: The nation replied to all this preaching of Shuaib with extreme arrogance, saying: O Shuaib, many of your words do not enter our understanding at all. The meaning is: these words of yours are highly absurd and far-fetched, otherwise who can be better than us in understanding reasonable words? After this, they gave the threat as well: you do not have any group and population that we should fear; we see you as highly weak and helpless among us. If it were not for your family, we would have stoned you. "and you are not against us noble"—that is, you yourself are not so heavy upon us that putting you away would be difficult for us; however, the thought of your family and tribe prevents us, why should we initiate a fight with them? The threat of stoning was given specifically because Shuaib used to condemn the idols of the nation; you will remember that on the condemnation of idols, this very threat was given to Abraham and other Prophets as well.

He said, "O my people, is my family more noble to you than Allah? And you have taken Him behind you as cast away. Indeed, my Lord, of what you do, is Encompassing."

Shuaib's Reliance upon Allah: Shuaib said in reply to this threat: O my people, is the fear and regard of my family and tribe more upon you than Allah, that you give importance to the family and tribe while you have cast God behind your backs? "Indeed, my Lord, of what you do, is Encompassing"—remember, my Lord encompasses all that you are doing or are about to do. There is no possibility that you can take any step without His permission. The meaning is: my reliance is upon my Lord; do what you want to do. "Zihriyyah" means that thing which is cast behind and forgotten.

And O my people, work according to your position; indeed, I am working. You are going to know to whom will come a punishment that will disgrace him and who is a liar. And watch; indeed, I am with you a watcher."

"Indeed, I am working"—meaning "indeed, I am working according to my position". "Makanah" means place and rank. That is: you work at your place, I work at my place. You do whatever you want to do in my opposition, and I will keep doing that mission of warning and giving glad tidings on which I have been appointed by my Lord. "You are going to know to whom will come a punishment that will disgrace him and who is a liar, and watch; indeed, I am with you a watcher"—you also wait, I also wait with you. That is, the knowledge of the unseen is not with me either, that I can tell with certainty at what time the punishment will come, but it is clear that all the signs of being deserving of punishment have manifested in you, which is why I too am now waiting for its sudden manifestation.

94–95. And when Our command came, We saved Shuaib and those who believed with him, by mercy from Us. And the shriek seized those who had wronged, and they became within their homes fallen prone. As if they had never prospered therein. Unquestionably, away with Madyan as Thamud was taken away.

In almost these very words, this same theme has passed in verses 66–68.

And We had certainly sent Moses with Our signs and a clear authority.

The Story of Moses and Pharaoh: Now in the following few verses, reference is made briefly to the story of Moses and Pharaoh. You will remember that in Surah Yunus, the story of Moses (peace be upon him) was narrated in detail while other Prophets were only briefly referred to. Contrary to this, in this Surah, the story of Moses is briefly referred to while other Prophets' stories, as you have seen, have been narrated in detail. In this way, both these Surahs join to complete each other, and this is the reality of the Surahs being in pairs. In the introduction of the book, we have discussed this issue. "with Our signs and a clear authority"—by "signs" (ayat) is meant those general signs which manifested at the hands of Moses at every step, and by "clear authority" (sultan mubeen) is meant specifically that sign through which Moses achieved clear victory over Pharaoh, his courtiers, and his sorcerers—namely, the miracle of the staff. Here, as it were, the mention of the specific is made after the general, and the reason for expressing it by the term "clear authority" is that its status was indeed of an overwhelming argument, after which all the prestige of Pharaoh and his courtiers was shattered in the eyes of their own people.

To Pharaoh and his establishment, but they followed the command of Pharaoh, and the command of Pharaoh was not discerning. That is: We sent Our Prophet for their guidance and direction, but these wretched people followed the opinion and command of Pharaoh. Pharaoh's leadership was not sound, because of which they fell into that ditch into which the followers of such leaders fall.

He will precede his people on the Day of Resurrection and lead them into the Fire. And wretched is the place to which they are led. "Wird" means descending upon some watering place to drink or make others drink. The meaning is: on the Day of Resurrection, this Pharaoh will be the leader of all those who followed him in this world, and he will lead them to the watering place of Hell, and there is no watering place worse than this.

And they were followed in this [world] with a curse and [as well] on the Day of Resurrection. Wretched is the gift which is given. "Rifd" means gift and reward. That is: in this world too, a curse was set behind them, and in the Hereafter too, this curse will remain set behind them, and how wretched is this gift which has been granted to them!

The Subject Ahead. Verses 100 to 123: Now ahead are the concluding verses of the Surah. In them, attention is drawn to those realities which are implicit within the stories of the past and to bring which before the addressee they were narrated. The lessons they contain for the Quraish have also been pointed to, and the instruction which the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his companions receive from them has also been referred to. Recite the verses ahead in this light.

"That is from the news of the cities which We relate to you; of them, some are [still] standing and some are [like] a harvest mown."

Reference to the Past Stories: Pointing towards those nations whose stories have been narrated above, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), and through his medium, his nation, were drawn attention: We are narrating these stories so that through them you also receive strength and courage, and the people of your nation also learn a lesson from them. This theme has been well clarified ahead in verse 120. "of them, some are standing and some are a harvest mown"—that is, of those cities, some are standing which you can see, and some of them have been completely obliterated. "Haseed" means mown harvest. Here, this has been used for those cities which were completely obliterated by divine punishment. An example of "standing" (qa'im) is Egypt, from where God extracted Pharaoh and his nation and drowned them in the sea; the places remained standing, the dwellers became non-existent. By "haseed" (mown) is meant the cities of the nation of 'Aad and the nation of Lot etc., whose places and dwellers all became non-existent, and only some ruins of someone remained to tell the story of the wretchedness of their dwellers. This was made heard to the Quraish because if they persist in this path of extreme opposition, they must inevitably write their name in one of these two lists. After the rise of the Messenger among them, their decision must also inevitably be made.

And We wronged them not, but they wronged themselves. And they were of no avail to them—their gods whom they called upon besides Allah—from anything when there came the command of your Lord. And they did not increase them other than in ruin. That is: do not think that We wronged them by destroying them. We did not commit any injustice upon them, but rather they wronged themselves. All the high capabilities with which Allah had created them were wasted by them, and by disobeying the commands of their Messenger, they themselves provided the causes of their destruction. "And they were of no avail to them—their gods whom they called upon besides Allah—from anything when there came the command of your Lord"—and when the command of God, namely the punishment, arrived, all those deities of theirs whom they worshipped besides God were of no use to them, because all of them were the invention of their illusion, and illusion is of no use against reality. "And they did not increase them other than in ruin"—that is, if they increased anything, they increased only in their destruction and ruin, because false supports increase only in misery and deprivation at the time of need. If a thirsty person runs towards a mirage considering it water, then at the time when the actual reality is made clear to him, death will be standing with open mouth from every side and all the paths of life will have been closed, whereas if he had not been involved in this misconception, it was possible that some path of saving life would have opened for him.

And thus is the seizure of your Lord when He seizes the cities while they are committing wrong. Indeed, His seizure is painful and severe.

Warning to the Quraish: That is: when God punishes nations and cities for their injustice, their rebellion, and their transgression, He punishes them in this very way as He punished those nations whose stories were narrated above. At that time, God's grip is extremely painful and severe. This was spoken addressing the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) so that the Quraish are warned that now their nation is also in the same scale in which other nations have been weighed. The dealing which God made with those nations, there is no reason why He should make any different dealing with them.

Indeed in that is a sign for those who fear the punishment of the Hereafter. That is a Day for which the people will be collected, and that is a Day [which will be] witnessed.

The Testimony of This World in Favor of the Hereafter: That is: these stories of the nations, just as they manifest the end of the denial of Prophets and Messengers, similarly within them is a very great sign for those who fear the punishment of the Hereafter. From these events, the attributes of the Creator and Master of this world come forward, and the standard of His likes and dislikes is determined—that when He inflicts exemplary punishments on the rebellious and the transgressors in this world, how is it possible that He has not kept any day of reward and punishment for this collective world? So it is necessary that a day of reward and punishment comes in which the good receive the reward of their goodness and the bad suffer the punishment of their badness. In other words, say thus: these events of history are testifying that this world is not a plaything of a player. Rather, this is a world made by a Just and Wise God, which is why it is necessary that after it comes such a day in which His perfect justice manifests and every good and bad person sees his goodness and badness with his own eyes. "That is a Day for which the people will be collected"—that is, this will be such a day in which all will be gathered together: those who passed before and those who came after, the Prophets and their nations, the callers to goodness and those who showed the paths of evil, the rulers and the ruled, the witnesses and the witnessed, so that every soul receives the reward of its goodness if it earned goodness, and suffers the punishment of its badness if it earned badness. "and that is a Day witnessed"—that is, this day will be of everyone's presence and hearing, so that all the parties of every matter are present before each other and their decision is made with full justice. This reality has been manifested in other places in the Quran as well. We refer to some verses so that its correct concept is established in the mind: "By the sky containing great stars, And [by] the promised Day, And [by] the witness and what is witnessed." (Al-Buruj: 1–3). "Indeed, We will support Our messengers and those who believe during the life of this world and on the Day when the witnesses will stand." (Ghafir: 51). "And [warn of] the Day when We will resurrect among every nation a witness over them from themselves." (An-Nahl: 89). "On a Day when their tongues, their hands and their feet will bear witness against them as to what they used to do." (An-Nur: 24).

And We do not delay it except for a limited term. That is: do not think that there is such an unlimited period remaining for the arrival of this day that currently there is no need to make one's enjoyment bitter in its apprehension. This period is not unlimited, rather it is a counted, limited term. "A counted term" is neither an exaggerated style of expression, nor has this been said from the perspective of divine knowledge; rather, this is the expression of an actual fact, because whoever dies, as is stated in the Hadith: "Whoever dies, his resurrection has already established." The period that will pass in the barzakh, at the time of the blowing of the trumpet, no feeling of it will remain. Every person will feel that they slept just now and have risen just now. When the actual reality is this, what doubt is there that in the arrival of resurrection, only as many days remain as many days of life remain. Life ended, resurrection arrived. As for the period of life, its being a counted, limited term is a self-evident matter.

The Day it comes no soul will speak except by His permission. And among them will be the wretched and the prosperous.

Refutation of False Intercession: When that hour comes, no one will have the power to move their tongue to speak without God's permission. This is the refutation of the polytheists' assumption—that their deities possess such a position of honor and trust with God that no matter what their deeds may be, they will somehow deliver their worshippers, and God will be compelled to listen to their intercession and accept their word to please them. Regarding the angels, their assumption was that these are God's beloved daughters, and just as beloved daughters make their father accept everything through pampering and demanding, these too will get all those forgiven who worshipped them in the world. This verse put an end to all these baseless and imaginary expectations. God's pampering anyone is far off; no one will even open their mouth before Him until He gives permission. In another place, there is also the explicit statement: whoever opens his mouth after God's permission will say only that which is right; he will not be able to deviate from the right even by a hair's breadth. "And among them will be the wretched and the prosperous"—that is, those who gather in the assembly of resurrection, some of them will be wretched and deprived of share, and some will be fortunate and successful. The first group will enter Hell, and the second group will be blessed with Paradise, and the wretchedness or fortunate state of each of them will be based on their deeds.

106–107. As for those who were wretched, they will be in the Fire, for them therein is sighing and wailing. Abiding therein as long as the heavens and the earth endure, except what your Lord should will. Indeed, your Lord is an effecter of what He intends.

The Expression of the Shrieking of the Dwellers of Hell: Those who are wretched will fall into Hell, and their state in it will be: "for them therein is sighing and wailing" (zafeerun wa shaheeq). Both words "zafeer" and "shaheeq" are used for the braying of a donkey when it brays; the breath it exhales outward is called "zafeer", and the breath it inhales inward is called "shaheeq". This is the expression of the shrieking, crying, and wailing of the dwellers of Hell, and the aspect of contempt present in these words is completely clear.

New Heaven, New Earth: "Abiding therein as long as the heavens and the earth endure"—they will remain in this Hell as long as the heavens and the earth endure. Obviously, by "heavens and earth" here, the current heavens and earth are not meant, because these heavens and earth will have ended at the time of the resurrection. Rather, those heavens and earth are meant which will manifest with new laws and rules at the time of the establishment of the resurrection, and to which allusion is made in the verse: "On the Day the earth will be replaced with another earth, and the heavens."

All Choice belongs to God: "except what your Lord should will"—except what your Lord should will. That is, no one else can deliver from this eternal punishment, but if your Lord Himself wills, He can reduce someone's punishment, or turn them into dust and ashes. "Indeed, your Lord is an effecter of what He intends"—your Lord can do what He wills; no one is there to hold His hand.

And as for those who were prosperous, they will be in Paradise, abiding therein as long as the heavens and the earth endure, except what your Lord should will—a gift uninterrupted.

Progress from Good to Better: Those who are fortunate will enter Paradise and will remain in it as long as the heavens and the earth endure. From the exception "except what your Lord should will", this allusion emerges: changes will occur in their states and ranks as well, but these changes will not be of the nature of good to evil, rather of the nature of good to better, because for them, there will never be an interruption in God's bounty. "a gift uninterrupted"—they will receive it as a gift that will never be cut off.

So be not in doubt, [O Muhammad], as to what these [polytheists] worship. They do not worship except as their fathers worshipped before. And indeed, We will give them their share undiminished.

Address to the Prophet, Wrath upon the Opponents: We have clarified this style at multiple places: sometimes the address apparently in words is to the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), but the wrath implicit within it is directed at the opponents. Since they, due to their obstinacy, are no longer worthy of address, the matter, instead of being spoken to them directly, is spoken to the Prophet. An example of this has passed in Surah Yunus, verses 94–95. The meaning is: let not their persistence in shirk cause you some misunderstanding—that they possess some proof for it; they are worshipping these stones and imaginary deities without understanding, exactly as their fathers worshipped before them. Neither did they take the trouble to use intellect and proof, nor do these want to do so. "And indeed, We will give them their share"—that is, let them live in whichever fool's paradise they want to live, We will fulfill their share. We will not decrease anything in it.

And We had certainly given Moses the Scripture, but it was differed on. And if not for a word that preceded from your Lord, it would have been judged between them. And indeed they are, about the Quran, in disquieting doubt.

This verse, in whichever context it is here, is in exactly this context in Surah Ha-Mim As-Sajdah. See verses 43–45: "Nothing is said to you, [O Muhammad], except what was already said to the messengers before you. Indeed, your Lord is a possessor of forgiveness and a possessor of painful retribution. And if We had made it a foreign Quran, they would have said, 'Why were its verses not explained in detail? [Is it] a foreign [recitation] and an Arab [messenger]?' Say, 'It is, for those who believe, a guidance and cure.' And those who do not believe—in their ears is deafness, and it is upon them blindness. Those are being called from a distant place. And We had certainly given Moses the Scripture, but it was differed on. And if not for a word that preceded from your Lord, it would have been judged between them. And indeed they are, about it, in disquieting doubt."

Here, the verse under discussion is in the position of consolation. The Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is reassured: just as the people of your nation have gone after you upon your presenting this Book, similarly the people of Moses' nation also disputed and differed with him at every step in the matter of the Torah. The meaning is: the situation you are facing is the very situation your predecessor Prophets faced; so just as they faced the circumstances with patience and steadfastness until Allah granted them success, similarly you also face the circumstances with patience and steadfastness. Allah will make you victorious over your opponents too. "And if not for a word that preceded from your Lord, it would have been judged between them"—that is, Allah has already appointed a time for everything beforehand. For this reason, every matter will occur according to His appointed program. For your nation too, a period of respite is appointed. When that period is complete, their measure will also be full, and the decision will be made between them. "And indeed they are, about the Quran, in disquieting doubt"—that is, these people are in a disquieting doubt regarding what you are presenting. "Mureeb" means disquieting. By bringing this attribute, the objective is to show that this path of denial they have adopted is based only on the desire not to believe; it has no foundation. What you are presenting, its proof is clear, but they do not want to believe. For this reason, they have become caught in a severe anxiety. Neither can they stay, nor can they go.

And indeed, each [of them]—your Lord will surely fully repay them for their deeds. Indeed, He, of what they do, is Acquainted.

In "lamma" (surely), "la" is for emphasis and oath, and "ma" is extra (za'id), which sometimes comes merely to keep the cadence and sound of the sentence proper. For example: "Indeed, all of that is surely (lamma) the enjoyment of worldly life." Similarly in Surah At-Tariq: "There is no soul but surely (lamma) over it is a guardian."

Although the word "kull" (each/all) is general, here it refers to those very groups who were mentioned above—namely, the polytheists of the Quraish and the Jews. It is said: let these people try whatever strength of their heel and toe they want to try in the opposition to the call of truth. That time will also come when your Lord will give them the full recompense of all their deeds. Of what they are doing, He is fully aware. It should be kept in mind that the stage of the call discussed here is when the Jews had also started backing the Quraish.

So remain firm as you have been commanded, [O Muhammad], and those who have turned back with you, and do not transgress. Indeed, He, of what you do, is Seeing. This is the exhortation to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and those who believed with him to remain upright on the path of truth: in this storm of oppositions, remain firmly established on that very path which your Lord has opened for you. "And do not transgress"—that is, being overawed by the circumstances or influenced by temptations, do not deviate from this path even slightly. "Indeed, He, of what you do, is Seeing"—whatever you are doing or will do, He is seeing all of it. He will guide you in difficulties, and when you are in need of His help, He will support you.

And do not incline toward those who do wrong, lest you be touched by the Fire, and you would not have besides Allah any protectors; then you would not be helped.

Exhortation to Those Who Believed on Steadfastness on the Path of Truth: This is a warning to those who believed in you: being influenced by fear or greed, do not incline towards those who have committed injustice—namely, shirk and disbelief; otherwise, that very fire of Hell will seize you too which is destined for them, and at that time, there will be no helper for you against God. "then you would not be helped"—that is, if you incline slightly towards these disbelievers and polytheists, you will remain deprived of that help which is being promised to you in this world. This promise is conditional on the condition of steadfastness. Without it, you cannot deserve God's help.

114–115. And establish prayer at the two ends of the day and at the approach of the night. Indeed, good deeds do away with misdeeds. That is a reminder for those who remember. And be patient, for indeed, Allah does not allow to be lost the reward of those who do good.

Prayer is the Means of Steadfastness: This is the device described for the acquisition of that patience and steadfastness which has been commanded in the verses above. It has been clarified at various places in the Quran that the spiritual strength to face the oppositions that arise from Satan and his helpers in the path of God is acquired only through prayer. This very thing connects the servant with God, and when the servant is connected with his Lord, the effusion of divine lights and blessings occurs upon him, the heart becomes clean of whispers and weaknesses, and he becomes ready to face the circumstances with full determination and courage. In Surah Ta-Ha, this mutual relationship of patience and prayer has been clarified thus: "So be patient over what they say and exalt [Allah] with praise of your Lord before the rising of the sun and before its setting; and during periods of the night exalt [Him] and at the ends of the day, that you may be satisfied." (Ta-Ha: 130).

In the verse under discussion, there is no detail of the times of prayer, but if you ponder over the aforementioned verse of Ta-Ha, in it there is not only an allusion to the times of the five obligatory prayers, but the times of Chasht and Tahajjud are also implicit in it; however, here we only want to clarify the mutual relationship of patience and prayer. We will discuss the issue of the times of prayer, God willing, at its place.

A Comprehensive Warning: "Indeed, good deeds do away with misdeeds" is a comprehensive general principle.
