The word "hasanaat" (good deeds) has gathered within itself prayer and all other virtues of its family. The meaning is: remain active as much as possible in these goodnesses alone, because these very things will erase those evils which can raise their head from within or from outside. "That is a reminder for those who remember"—the value and worth of this instruction has been drawn attention to: do not consider this an ordinary matter. This is a very valuable admonition for those who listen and understand. This warning was necessary because it was not easy for everyone to understand that prayer is also the greatest weapon of struggle in the path of truth.

The Patience of Those Who Do Good: "And be patient, for indeed, Allah does not allow to be lost the reward of those who do good"—to strengthen this patience and make it unshakeable, in the verse above, as we indicated, prayer is commanded. In the mention of steadfastness before prayer and patience after prayer, there is also an allusion to this reality: perseverance and steadfastness are praiseworthy only when a person's direction is towards the correct path. In addition, there is another point in this verse. It is that it was not said thus: "and be patient, for indeed, Allah does not allow to be lost the reward of those who are patient." Rather, it was said thus: "and be patient, for indeed, Allah does not allow to be lost the reward of those who do good (muhsineen)." From this, the objective is to point to the reality that patience is beloved and worthy of reward with God only of those people who perform patience in the way patience is deserved to be performed. Everyone performs patience by weeping, crying, and complaining. The patience of those who do good is that saws are run over their heads, but neither does the heart complain, nor does any wrinkle appear on the forehead. Those who perform patience with this dignity and gravity, their reward is never lost. On the word "ihsan" (doing good), we have discussed in another place and shown that its meaning is also to perform some work with extreme beauty.

So why were there not among the generations before you providers of virtue forbidding corruption on earth, except a few of those We saved from among them? But those who wronged followed what luxury they had been given therein, and they were criminals.

The Meaning of "Oloo Baqiyyah": That is: the virtuous and righteous, and standard-bearers of truth. In Arabic, when they say "Fulanun baqiyyatu qawmihi", its meaning is: he is among the virtuous and righteous of his nation.

"Ma utrifoo feehi"—"atrafahu-al-mal" means: wealth and resources involved him in transgression and corruption. That is: they remained lying in that very intoxication in which the abundance of means of luxury and comfort had involved them.

Warning to the Quraish: Now, pointing to those punished nations whose mention has passed above, it is stated what thing became the cause of their destruction, and the objective of this is to warn the Quraish that if you adopt the same path, there is no reason why your end should be different. It is said: there did not remain among them such people who could forbid people from spreading corruption on God's earth. Their majority consisted of such people who remained absorbed in that very intoxication in which they were involved. Only a few people among them emerged who were God-fearing. So We saved them. As for the rest who committed injustice on their own souls, they followed their desires and were destroyed, because they were criminals.

And your Lord would not have destroyed the cities unjustly while their people were reformers.

The Divine Sunnah regarding the Destruction of Nations: Now, the Divine sunnah regarding the destruction of nations is clarified: it is not Allah Almighty's way to destroy any town in retribution of some injustice of theirs while its inhabitants are, collectively, reformers. The meaning is: God's punishment comes over any nation when the temperament of the nation, collectively, becomes corrupt. Those who reform either do not remain in it at all, or if they remain, they are very few in number. The word "qura" (cities) here is in the concept of nations, and from the indefiniteness of "bi-zulmin" (unjustly/for an injustice), the objective is to clarify the reality that individual evils do not become the cause of divine punishment. Divine punishment descends only when the temperament of the collective becomes corrupt.

118–119. And if your Lord had willed, He could have made mankind one community; but they will not cease to differ. Except whom your Lord has given mercy, and for that He created them. And the word of your Lord has been filled: 'I will surely fill Hell with jinn and men all together.'

The Divine Law in the Matter of Guidance and Misguidance: That is: if Allah had wanted to make everyone a single community, He could have done so; no one was there to hold His hand. But He did not prefer this compulsion in the matter of guidance and misguidance; rather, He placed both goodness and evil before people along with the details of their end and gave them choice to adopt whichever of them they want. If they adopt the path of goodness, its reward is the successes of the eternal life of the Hereafter, and if they adopt the path of evil, they will suffer its punishment in the Hereafter. "but they will not cease to differ"—that is, when Allah did not prefer compulsion in this matter, rather gave people choice, then it should not be expected that every person will adopt the path of goodness alone; rather, many among them will keep emerging who, despite all teaching and reminding, in the following of their desire and Satan, will adopt the path of evil and will live on it, and die on it. "Except whom your Lord has given mercy"—that is, only those people will remain protected from adopting the path of evil upon whom is your Lord's mercy. This matter is implicit within the context of the speech here: those alone can deserve the mercy of God who make use of their capabilities of hearing, seeing, intellect, and heart, and do not stumble despite having eyes. "and for that He created them"—that is, Allah created people for this very reason: that they, by their choice and will, make themselves deserving of their Lord's favor and mercy. This trial is an essential part of the creation of man. Without passing through this, no person can become deserving of the mercy of God. "And the word of your Lord has been filled: 'I will surely fill Hell with jinn and men all together.'"—that is, those who fail in this trial, the word of your Lord will be fulfilled in their favor: "I will fill Hell with jinn and humans." This is the reference to that statement of Allah Almighty which was spoken in response to Iblis, and the detail of which is in another place: Iblis, while refusing to prostrate to Adam, said to Allah Almighty: "I will cast such nets on the progeny of Adam that You will not find most of them grateful to You." Allah Almighty said in response to his threat: "I will fill Hell with all such jinn and all such humans."

And each [story] We relate to you of the news of the messengers is that by which We make firm your heart. And there has come to you in this, the truth and an instruction and a reminder for the believers.

The Objective of Narrating the Stories: Pointing towards those stories of the Prophets (peace be upon them) and their nations narrated above, it is said: We narrate these stories so that We bring those aspects of theirs which make your heart firm, so that you can face the circumstances with patience and steadfastness which you are facing or may face in the future. And rest assured that the history of the victory of truth and the defeat of falsehood which has come before you from these events is entirely based on realities. This is how it has happened, and this is how it will happen. This is based on the divine sunnah, and there can be no change in the divine sunnah. "and an instruction and a reminder for the believers"—and in these stories, there is also instruction and reminder for those who have believed in you. The things they need to avoid in this storm of oppositions are also clarified by them, and the path they should adopt also comes forward.

121–122. And say to those who do not believe, 'Work according to your position; indeed, we are working. And wait; indeed, we are waiting.'

The Decisive Response to the Opponents: Now this is a decisive response at the end to the opponents: do what you want to do in our opposition, and we too will keep doing that work of the call to truth in spite of all your oppositions and all your absurdities. If you are demanding punishment, then wait for it, we too are waiting for it. Because all those signs which make a nation deserving of punishment are manifesting in you one by one, but sending the punishment is the work of the Knower of the unseen. He alone knows when your decision should be made.

And to Allah belongs the unseen of the heavens and the earth and to Him will be returned the matter, all of it. So worship Him and rely upon Him. And your Lord is not unaware of that which you do.

Instruction to Rely upon Allah: This is the instruction at the end to commit the entire matter to Allah, to remain active with all one's body and soul in His worship, and to rely upon Him fully—that the entire secret of the heavens and the earth is in Allah's knowledge and choice, and all matters are presented before Him for decision, so you worship Him alone and keep reliance on Him. Your Lord is not unaware of what you are doing. He is with you at every stage and every step. He will guide you and help you in every difficulty.

These are the final lines which this humble and helpless servant was granted the ability to commit to paper in the commentary of this Surah. And our final call is that all praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of the worlds.
